**Maldives**,[[c]](#cite_note-10) officially the **Republic of Maldives**,[[d]](#cite_note-11) and historically known as the **Maldive Islands**, is an [archipelagic country](/wiki/Archipelagic_state "Archipelagic state") in [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), located in the eastern [Arabian Sea](/wiki/Arabian_Sea "Arabian Sea"), within the northern [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"). Maldives is southwest of [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") and [India](/wiki/India "India"), about 750 kilometres (470 miles; 400 nautical miles) from the Asian continent's mainland. Maldives' chain of [26 atolls](/wiki/Atolls_of_the_Maldives "Atolls of the Maldives") stretches across the equator from [Ihavandhippolhu Atoll](/wiki/Atolls_of_the_Maldives#Ihavandhippolhu "Atolls of the Maldives") in the north to [Addu Atoll](/wiki/Addu_Atoll "Addu Atoll") in the south.

Maldives is the smallest and extremely remote [country in Asia](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_and_dependent_territories_in_Asia "List of sovereign states and dependent territories in Asia"). Its land area is only 298 square kilometres (115 sq mi), but this is spread over roughly 90,000 square kilometres (35,000 sq mi) of the sea, making it one of the world's most spatially dispersed sovereign states. With a population of 515,132 in the 2022 census, it is the second [least populous country in Asia](/wiki/List_of_Asian_countries_by_population "List of Asian countries by population") and the [ninth-smallest country by area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_area "List of countries and dependencies by area"), but also one of the [most densely populated countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population_density "List of countries and dependencies by population density"). Maldives has an average ground-level elevation of around 1.5 metres (4 ft 11 in) above sea level,[[8]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-12) and a [highest natural point](/wiki/List_of_elevation_extremes_by_country "List of elevation extremes by country") of only 2.4 metres (7 ft 10 in), making it the world's lowest-lying country. Some sources state the highest point, [Mount Villingili](/wiki/Mount_Villingili "Mount Villingili"), as 5.1 metres or 17 feet.[[8]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-12)

[Malé](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9 "Malé") is the capital and the most populated city, traditionally called the "King's Island", where the ancient [royal dynasties](/wiki/List_of_Maldivian_monarchs "List of Maldivian monarchs") ruled from its central location.[[9]](#cite_note-13) Maldives has been inhabited for over 2,500 years. Documented contact with the outside world began around 947 AD when [Arab](/wiki/Arab "Arab") travellers began visiting the islands. In the 12th century, partly due to the importance of the Arabs and [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians") as traders in the Indian Ocean, Islam reached the Maldivian Archipelago.[[10]](#cite_note-14) Maldives was soon consolidated as a [sultanate](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Maldives "Sultanate of Maldives"), developing strong commercial and cultural ties with Asia and Africa. From the mid-16th century, the region came under the increasing influence of European [colonial powers](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism"), with Maldives becoming a British [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate") in 1887. [Independence from the United Kingdom](/wiki/Independence_of_Maldives "Independence of Maldives") came in 1965, and a [presidential republic](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") was established in 1968 with an elected People's Majlis. The ensuing decades have seen political instability, efforts at democratic reform,[[11]](#cite_note-15) and environmental challenges posed by [climate change and rising sea levels](/wiki/Climate_change_in_the_Maldives "Climate change in the Maldives").[[12]](#cite_note-16) Maldives became a founding member of the [South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation") (SAARC).

[Fishing](/wiki/Fishing_industry_in_the_Maldives "Fishing industry in the Maldives") has historically been the dominant economic activity, and remains the second largest sector, behind the rapidly growing [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_Maldives "Tourism in the Maldives") industry. Maldives rates "high" on the [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"),[[13]](#cite_note-HDI-17) with a *per capita* income significantly higher than other SAARC nations.[[14]](#cite_note-18) The World Bank classifies Maldives as having an upper-[middle income](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") economy.[[15]](#cite_note-19)

Maldives is a member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation"), and the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), and is a Dialogue Partner of the [Shanghai Cooperation Organisation](/wiki/Shanghai_Cooperation_Organisation "Shanghai Cooperation Organisation").[[16]](#cite_note-20) It temporarily withdrew from the Commonwealth in October 2016 after being threatened with expulsion from the organisation for its human rights infringements and democratic backsliding.[[17]](#cite_note-21) It was readmitted to the Commonwealth on 1 February 2020 after showing evidence of reform and functioning democratic processes.[[18]](#cite_note-22)

## Etymology

See also: [Names of the Maldives](/wiki/Names_of_the_Maldives "Names of the Maldives")

According to legends, the first settlers of Maldives were people known as Dheyvis.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23) The first Kingdom of Maldives was known as Kingdom of Dheeva Maari.[[20]](#cite_note-24) During the 3rd century BCE visit of emissaries, it was noted that Maldives was known as Dheeva Mahal.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23)

During c. 1100 – 1166, Maldives was also referred to as Diva Kudha, and the Laccadive [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") which was a part of Maldives was then referred to as Diva Kanbar by the scholar and polymath [al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni").[[21]](#cite_note-25)

The name [*Maldives*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Maldives "wikt:Maldives") may also derive from [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") माला *mālā* ([garland](/wiki/Garland "Garland")) and द्वीप *dvīpa* (island),[[22]](#cite_note-Hogendorn-26) or මාල දිවයින *Maala Divaina* ("Necklace Islands") in [Sinhala](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language").[[23]](#cite_note-ParanavitanaPrematilleka1978-27) The Maldivian people are called *Dhivehin*. The word *Dheeb/Deeb* (archaic Dhivehi, related to [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") द्वीप, *dvīpa*) means "island", and *Dhives* (*Dhivehin*) means "islanders" (i.e., Maldivians).[[24]](#cite_note-28) In Tamil, "Garland of Islands" can be translated as *Mālaitīvu* (மாலைத்தீவு).[[25]](#cite_note-29)

The venerable Sri Lankan chronicle Mahavamsa mentions an island designated as *Mahiladiva* ("Island of Women", महिलादिभ) in Pali, likely arising from an erroneous translation of the Sanskrit term, signifying "garland".

Jan Hogendorn, professor of economics at [Colby College](/wiki/Colby_College "Colby College"), theorised that the name Maldives derives from the Sanskrit *mālādvīpa* (मालाद्वीप), meaning "garland of islands".[[22]](#cite_note-Hogendorn-26) In [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), "Garland of Islands" can be translated as *Maladweepu* (മാലദ്വീപ്).[[26]](#cite_note-30) In Kannada, "Garland of Islands" can be translated as *Maaledweepa* (ಮಾಲೆದ್ವೀಪ).[[27]](#cite_note-31) None of these names are mentioned in any literature, however, classical Sanskrit texts dating back to the [Vedic period](/wiki/Vedic_period "Vedic period") mention the "Hundred Thousand Islands" (*[Lakshadweepa](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep")*), a generic name which would include not only Maldives, but also the [Laccadives](/wiki/Laccadives "Laccadives"), [Aminidivi](/wiki/Aminidivi "Aminidivi") Islands, [Minicoy](/wiki/Minicoy "Minicoy"), and the [Chagos island](/wiki/Chagos_Archipelago "Chagos Archipelago") groups.[[28]](#cite_note-32)[[29]](#cite_note-33)

Medieval Muslim travellers such as [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta") called the islands **Maḥal Dībīyāt** (محل ديبية) from the Arabic word **maḥal** ("palace"), which must be how the [Berber](/wiki/Berber_people "Berber people") traveller interpreted the name of Malé, having been through Muslim North India, where [Perso-Arabic](/wiki/Perso-Arabic "Perso-Arabic") words were introduced to the local vocabulary.[[30]](#cite_note-34) This is the name currently inscribed on the scroll in Maldives [state emblem](/wiki/Emblem_of_Maldives "Emblem of Maldives").[[31]](#cite_note-MVEmblems-35) The classical Persian/Arabic name for Maldives is *Dibajat*.[[32]](#cite_note-36)[[33]](#cite_note-37) The Dutch referred to the islands as the *Maldivische Eilanden* (pronounced [[mɑlˈdivisə ˈʔɛilɑndə(n)]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch")),[[34]](#cite_note-WWW.IBPUS.COM_69-38) while the British [anglicised](/wiki/Anglicised "Anglicised") the local name for the islands first to the "Maldive Islands" and later to "Maldives".[[34]](#cite_note-WWW.IBPUS.COM_69-38)

In a conversational book published in 1563, [Garcia de Orta](/wiki/Garcia_de_Orta "Garcia de Orta") writes: "I must tell you that I have heard it said that the natives do not call it Maldiva but Nalediva. In the Malabar language, *nale* means four, and *diva* means island. So that in that language, the word signifies 'four islands', while we, corrupting the name, call it Maldiva."[[35]](#cite_note-39)

The local name for Maldives by the Maldivian people in [Dhivehi](/wiki/Maldivian_language "Maldivian language") language is *Dhivehi Raajje*, (ދިވެހިރާއްޖެ).[[36]](#cite_note-40)

## History

Main article: [History of the Maldives](/wiki/History_of_the_Maldives "History of the Maldives")

### Ancient history and settlement

Main article: [History of the Maldives § Early Age](/wiki/History_of_the_Maldives#Early_Age "History of the Maldives")

In the 6th–5th century BCE, kingdoms already existed in the modern-day Maldives.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23) The country has an established history of over 2,500 years according to historical evidence and legends.[[37]](#cite_note-41)

The *[Mahāvaṃsa](/wiki/Mah%C4%81va%E1%B9%83sa "Mahāvaṃsa")* (300 BCE) has records of people from Sri Lanka emigrating to Maldives.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23) Assuming that cowrie shells come from Maldives, historians believe that there may have been people living in Maldives during the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") (3300–1300 BCE).[[38]](#cite_note-42) Several artefacts show the presence of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") in the country before the Islamic period.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23)

According to the book *Kitāb fi āthār Mīdhu al-qādimah* (كتاب في آثار ميذو القديمة) (*On the Ancient Ruins of Meedhoo*), written in the 17th century in Arabic by Allama Ahmed Shihabuddine (Allama Shihab al-Din) of [Meedhoo](/wiki/Meedhoo_(Addu) "Meedhoo (Addu)") in Addu Atoll, the first settlers of Maldives were people known as Dheyvis.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23) They are said to be from [Kalibangan](/wiki/Kalibangan "Kalibangan") in India.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23) The time of their arrival is unknown but before Emperor [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka")'s kingdom in 269–232 BCE. Shihabuddin's story tallies remarkably well with the recorded history of South Asia and that of the copperplate document of Maldives known as [Lōmāfānu](/wiki/L%C5%8Dm%C4%81f%C4%81nu "Lōmāfānu").[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23)

The ancient history of Maldives is told in copperplates, ancient scripts carved on coral artefacts, traditions, language, and different ethnicities of Maldivians.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23) The *Maapanansa*,[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23) the copper plates on which the history of the first [kings of Maldives](/wiki/List_of_Maldivian_monarchs "List of Maldivian monarchs") from the Solar Dynasty was recorded, were lost early.

A 4th-century notice written by [Ammianus Marcellinus](/wiki/Ammianus_Marcellinus "Ammianus Marcellinus") (362 CE) speaks of gifts sent to the Roman emperor [Julian](/wiki/Julian_(emperor) "Julian (emperor)") by a deputation from the nation of Divi. The name Divi is very similar to Dheyvi who were the first settlers of Maldives.[[19]](#cite_note-:12-23)

The first Maldivians did not leave any archaeological artefacts. Their buildings were probably built of wood, palm fronds, and other perishable materials, which would have quickly decayed in the salt and wind of the tropical climate. Moreover, chiefs or headmen did not reside in elaborate stone palaces, nor did their religion require the construction of large temples or compounds.[[39]](#cite_note-43)

Comparative studies of Maldivian oral, linguistic, and cultural traditions confirm that the first settlers were people from the southern shores of the neighbouring [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"),[[40]](#cite_note-autogenerated2-44) including the [Giraavaru people](/wiki/Giraavaru_people "Giraavaru people"), mentioned in ancient legends and local folklore about the establishment of the capital and kingly rule in Malé.[[41]](#cite_note-45)

A strong underlying layer of [Dravidian](/wiki/Dravidian_peoples "Dravidian peoples") and [North Indian](/wiki/North_Indian_culture "North Indian culture") cultures survives in Maldivian society, with a clear [Elu](/wiki/Elu "Elu") substratum in the language, which also appears in place names, kinship terms, poetry, dance, and religious beliefs.[[42]](#cite_note-Maloney,_Clarence-46) The North Indian system was brought by the original [Sinhalese](/wiki/Sinhalese_people "Sinhalese people") from [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"). [Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_region "Malabar region") and [Pandya](/wiki/Pandya "Pandya") seafaring culture led to the settlement of the Islands by [Tamil](/wiki/Tamils "Tamils") and [Malabar](/wiki/Malabars "Malabars") seafarers.[[42]](#cite_note-Maloney,_Clarence-46)

### Buddhist period

Main articles: [History of the Maldives § Buddhist period](/wiki/History_of_the_Maldives#Buddhist_period "History of the Maldives"), and [Buddhism in the Maldives](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_Maldives "Buddhism in the Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Loamaafaanu.jpg/250px-Loamaafaanu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Loamaafaanu.jpg)

[Isdhoo](/wiki/Isdhoo_(Laamu_Atoll) "Isdhoo (Laamu Atoll)") Lōmāfānu is the oldest [copper-plate](/wiki/L%C5%8Dm%C4%81f%C4%81nu "Lōmāfānu") book to have been discovered in Maldives to date. The book was written in 1194 CE (590 AH) in the Evēla form of the [Divehi akuru](/wiki/Dhivehi_Writing_Systems "Dhivehi Writing Systems"), during the reign of [Siri Fennaadheettha Mahaa Radun (Dhinei Kalaminja)](/wiki/Dhinei_of_the_Maldives "Dhinei of the Maldives").

Despite being just mentioned briefly in most history books, the 1,400 year-long Buddhist period has a foundational importance in the history of Maldives. It was during this period that the culture of Maldives both developed and flourished, a culture that survives today. The Maldivian [language](/wiki/Dhivehi_language "Dhivehi language"), early Maldive scripts, architecture, ruling institutions, customs, and manners of the Maldivians originated at the time when Maldives were a Buddhist kingdom.[[43]](#cite_note-47)

Buddhism probably spread to Maldives in the 3rd century BCE at the time of Emperor [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka")'s expansion and became the dominant religion of the people of Maldives until the 12th century. Archeological evidence from an ancient Buddhist monastery in [Kaashidhoo](/wiki/Kaashidhoo_(Kaafu_Atoll) "Kaashidhoo (Kaafu Atoll)") has been dated between 205 and 560 AD, based on the radiocarbon dating of shell deposits unearthed from the foundations of [stupas](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa") and other structures in the monastery.[[44]](#cite_note-mikkelsen2000-48) The ancient Maldivian Kings promoted [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), and the first Maldive writings and artistic achievements, in the form of highly developed sculpture and architecture, originate from that period. Nearly all archaeological remains in Maldives are from Buddhist [stupas](/wiki/Stupa "Stupa") and monasteries, and all artefacts found to date display characteristic Buddhist iconography.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Islamic period

See also: [History of the Maldives § Islamic Period](/wiki/History_of_the_Maldives#Islamic_Period "History of the Maldives"), [Islam in Maldives](/wiki/Islam_in_Maldives "Islam in Maldives"), [List of Maldivian monarchs](/wiki/List_of_Maldivian_monarchs "List of Maldivian monarchs"), and [Sultanate of the Maldive Islands](/wiki/Sultanate_of_the_Maldive_Islands "Sultanate of the Maldive Islands")

The importance of the Arabs as traders in the Indian Ocean by the 12th century may partly explain why the last Buddhist king of Maldives, [Dhovemi](/wiki/Dhovemi_of_the_Maldives "Dhovemi of the Maldives"), converted to Islam in 1153 (or 1193). Adopting the Muslim title of Sultan Muhammad al-Adil, he initiated a series of six Islamic dynasties that lasted until 1932 when the [sultanate](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Maldives "Sultanate of Maldives") became elective. The formal title of the sultan up to 1965 was, *Sultan of Land and Sea, Lord of the twelve-thousand islands and Sultan of Maldives* which came with the style *[Highness](/wiki/Highness "Highness")*.

A Moroccan traveller named Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari is traditionally cited for this conversion.[[45]](#cite_note-:2-49) According to the story told to [Ibn Battutah](/wiki/Ibn_Battutah "Ibn Battutah"), a mosque was built with the inscription: 'The Sultan Ahmad Shanurazah accepted Islam at the hand of Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari.'[[45]](#cite_note-:2-49)[[46]](#cite_note-50) The folk tale regarding the spread of Islam by al-Barbari involved a local belief to an oceanic deity named [Rannamaari](/wiki/Rannamaari "Rannamaari"). According to the legend, Rannamaari demanded sacrifice of a female virgin each month from the locals. This tradition practiced for long time, until al-Barbari visit Maldives and managed to convert the local ruler and convinced him along with his subjects to abandon the Rannamari worship.[[47]](#cite_note-StephanieSmith-51)[[48]](#cite_note-XavierRannamari-52) According to modern [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegia "Norwegia") archaeologist [Egil Mikkelsen](/w/index.php?title=Egil_Mikkelsen&action=edit&redlink=1 "Egil Mikkelsen (page does not exist)") [[no](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/MikkelsenRannaanari "no:MikkelsenRannaanari")], it is likely the myth of Rannamari was heavily influenced by [Buddhist tantric](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") rituals and beliefs that were practiced in the islands at the time.[[49]](#cite_note-MikkelsenRannaanari-53)

Some scholars have suggested the possibility of Ibn Battuta misreading Maldive texts, and having a bias towards the North African, Maghrebi narrative of this Shaykh, instead of the Persian origins account that was known as well at the time.[[50]](#cite_note-54) Others have it that he may have been from the Persian town of [Tabriz](/wiki/Tabriz "Tabriz").[[51]](#cite_note-auto-55) This interpretation, held by the more reliable local historical chronicles, Raadavalhi and Taarikh,[[52]](#cite_note-Visweswaran2011-56)[[53]](#cite_note-sw-57) is that Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari was Abdul Barakat Yusuf Shams ud-Dīn at-Tabrīzī, also locally known as Tabrīzugefānu.[[54]](#cite_note-58) In the Arabic script the words al-Barbari and al-Tabrizi are very much alike, since at the time, Arabic had several consonants that looked identical and could only be differentiated by overall context (this has since changed by the addition of dots above or below letters to clarify pronunciation. For example, the letter "B" in modern Arabic has a dot below, whereas the letter "T" looks identical except there are two dots above it). "ٮوسڡ الٮٮرٮرى‎" could be read as "Yusuf at-Tabrizi" or "Yusuf al-Barbari".[[55]](#cite_note-59)

The venerated tomb of the scholar now stands on the grounds of [Medhu Ziyaaraiy](/wiki/Muliaage#Medhu_Ziyaaraiy "Muliaage"), across the street from the Friday Mosque, or [Hukuru Miskiy](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9_Friday_Mosque "Malé Friday Mosque"), in Malé. Originally built in 1153 and re-built in 1658,[[56]](#cite_note-Yoosuf-60) this is one of the oldest surviving mosques in Maldives. Following the Islamic concept that before Islam there was the time of [Jahiliya](/wiki/Jahiliyyah "Jahiliyyah") (ignorance), in the history books used by Maldivians the [introduction of Islam](#Introduction_of_Islam) at the end of the 12th century is considered the cornerstone of the country's history. Nonetheless, the cultural influence of Buddhism remains, a reality directly experienced by Ibn Battuta during his nine months there sometime between 1341 and 1345, serving as a chief judge and marrying into the royal family of [Omar I](/wiki/Omar_I_of_the_Maldives "Omar I of the Maldives").[[57]](#cite_note-61) For he became embroiled in local politics and left when his strict judgements in the laissez-faire island kingdom began to chafe with its rulers. In particular, he was angered at the local women going about with no clothing above the waist— a cultural epithet of the region at the time- was seen as a violation of Middle Eastern Islamic rules of modesty—and the locals taking no notice when he complained.[[58]](#cite_note-62)

Compared to the other areas of South Asia, the conversion of Maldives to Islam happened relatively late. Maldives remained a Buddhist kingdom for another 500 years. Arabic became the prime language of administration (instead of Persian and Urdu), and the [Maliki](/wiki/Maliki "Maliki") school of jurisprudence was introduced, both hinting at direct contact with the core of the Arab world.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Middle Eastern seafarers had just begun to take over the Indian Ocean trade routes in the 10th century and found Maldives to be an important link in those routes as the first landfall for traders from [Basra](/wiki/Basra "Basra") sailing to Southeast Asia. Trade involved mainly [cowrie shells](/wiki/Cowrie_shells "Cowrie shells")—widely used as a form of currency throughout Asia and parts of the East African coast—and coir fibre. The [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate"), where cowrie shells were used as legal tender, was one of the principal trading partners of Maldives. The Bengal–Maldives cowry shell trade was the largest shell currency trade network in history.[[59]](#cite_note-63)

The other essential product of Maldives was [coir](/wiki/Coir "Coir"), the fibre of the dried [coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut") [husk](/wiki/Husk "Husk"), resistant to saltwater. It stitched together and rigged the [dhows](/wiki/Dhow "Dhow") that plied the Indian Ocean. Maldivian coir was exported to [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh"), [China](/wiki/China "China"), [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"), and the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf").

### Protectorate period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/ConstantinoBraganca.jpg/250px-ConstantinoBraganca.jpg)](/wiki/File:ConstantinoBraganca.jpg)

[Portuguese presence](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire") in Maldives was established in 1558, by order of [Constantino of Braganza](/wiki/Constantino_of_Braganza "Constantino of Braganza"), Viceroy of [Portuguese India](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India").

In 1558, the Portuguese established a small garrison with a *Viador* (*Viyazoaru*), or overseer of a [factory (trading post)](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") in Maldives, which they administered from their main colony in [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa"). Their attempts to forcefully impose Christianity with the threat of death provoked a local revolt led by [Muhammad Thakurufaanu al-A'uẓam](/wiki/Muhammad_Thakurufaanu_al-Auzam "Muhammad Thakurufaanu al-Auzam"), his two brothers, and [Dhuvaafaru](/wiki/Dhuvaafaru "Dhuvaafaru") Dhandahele, who fifteen years later drove the Portuguese out of Maldives. This event is now commemorated as [Qaumee Dhuvas](/wiki/Qaumee_Dhuvas "Qaumee Dhuvas") (lit. 'National Day'). It is celebrated on the 1st of [Rabi' al-Awwal](/wiki/Rabi%27_al-Awwal "Rabi' al-Awwal"), the third month of [Hijri](/wiki/Islamic_Calendar "Islamic Calendar") (Islamic) calendar.[[60]](#cite_note-qaumiyyath-64)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Codice_Casanatense_Maldivans.jpg/250px-Codice_Casanatense_Maldivans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Codice_Casanatense_Maldivans.jpg)

16th-century Portuguese illustration from the [Códice Casanatense](/wiki/C%C3%B3dice_Casanatense "Códice Casanatense"), depicting workers

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/18th-century_Maldives_map_by_Pierre_Mortier.jpg/250px-18th-century_Maldives_map_by_Pierre_Mortier.jpg)](/wiki/File:18th-century_Maldives_map_by_Pierre_Mortier.jpg)

18th-century map by [Pierre Mortier](/wiki/Pierre_Mortier "Pierre Mortier") from the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), depicting with detail the islands of Maldives

In the mid-17th century, the Dutch, who had replaced the Portuguese as the dominant power in [Ceylon](/wiki/Ceylon "Ceylon"), established hegemony over Maldivian affairs without involving themselves directly in local matters, which were governed according to centuries-old Islamic customs.

The British expelled the Dutch from Ceylon in 1796 and included Maldives as a [British protectorate](/wiki/British_protectorate "British protectorate"). The status of Maldives as a British protectorate was officially recorded in an 1887 agreement in which the sultan [Muhammad Mueenuddeen II](/wiki/Muhammad_Mueenuddeen_II "Muhammad Mueenuddeen II") accepted British influence over Maldivian external relations and defence while retaining home rule, which continued to be regulated by [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") traditional institutions in exchange for an [annual tribute](/wiki/Maldivian_annual_tribute "Maldivian annual tribute"). The status of the islands was akin to other British protectorates in the Indian Ocean region, including [Zanzibar](/wiki/Zanzibar "Zanzibar") and the [Trucial States](/wiki/Trucial_States "Trucial States").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Maldivas_xvi.jpg/250px-Maldivas_xvi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maldivas_xvi.jpg)

17th-century Portuguese drawing of the fortress of Maldives and the archipelago. In Antonio Bocarro's book of Fortress (1632)[[61]](#cite_note-65)

In the British period, the Sultan's powers were taken over by the Chief Minister, much to the chagrin of the British Governor-General who continued to deal with the ineffectual Sultan. Consequently, Britain encouraged the development of a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), and the first Constitution was proclaimed in 1932. However, the new arrangements favoured neither the Sultan nor the Chief Minister, but rather a young crop of British-educated reformists. As a result, angry mobs were instigated against the Constitution which was publicly torn up.

Maldives remained a British crown protectorate until 1953 when the sultanate was suspended and the First Republic was declared under the short-lived presidency of [Mohamed Amin Didi](/wiki/Mohamed_Amin_Didi "Mohamed Amin Didi"). While serving as prime minister during the 1940s, Didi nationalised the fish export industry. As president, he is remembered as a reformer of the education system and an advocate of [women's rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights"). Conservatives in Malé ousted his government, and during a riot over food shortages, Didi was beaten by a mob and died on a nearby island.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Royal_Air_Force_Operations_in_the_Far_East%2C_1941-1945_CF620.jpg/250px-Royal_Air_Force_Operations_in_the_Far_East%2C_1941-1945_CF620.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Air_Force_Operations_in_the_Far_East,_1941-1945_CF620.jpg)

An [RAF](/wiki/RAF "RAF") [Short Sunderland](/wiki/Short_Sunderland "Short Sunderland") moored in the lagoon at Addu Atoll, during WWII

Beginning in the 1950s, the political history in Maldives was largely influenced by the British military presence on the islands. In 1954, the restoration of the sultanate perpetuated the rule of the past. Two years later, the United Kingdom obtained permission to reestablish its wartime [RAF Gan](/wiki/RAF_Gan "RAF Gan") airfield in the southernmost Addu Atoll, employing hundreds of locals. In 1957, however, the new prime minister, [Ibrahim Nasir](/wiki/Ibrahim_Nasir "Ibrahim Nasir"), called for a review of the agreement. Nasir was challenged in 1959 by a local secessionist movement in the three southernmost atolls that benefited economically from the British presence on [Gan](/wiki/Gan_(Addu_Atoll) "Gan (Addu Atoll)"). This group cut ties with Maldives government and formed an independent state, the [United Suvadive Republic](/wiki/United_Suvadive_Republic "United Suvadive Republic") with [Abdullah Afeef](/wiki/Abdullah_Afeef "Abdullah Afeef") as president and [Hithadhoo](/wiki/Hithadhoo_(Seenu_Atoll) "Hithadhoo (Seenu Atoll)") as its capital. One year later the Suvadive Republic was scrapped after Nasir sent gunboats from Malé with government police, and Abdullah Afeef went into exile. Meanwhile, in 1960 Maldives allowed the United Kingdom to continue to use both the [Gan](/wiki/RAF_Gan "RAF Gan") and the Hithadhoo facilities for thirty years, with the payment of £750,000 from 1960 to 1965 for Maldives' economic development. The base was closed in 1976 as part of the larger British withdrawal of permanently stationed forces '[East of Suez](/wiki/East_of_Suez "East of Suez")'.[[62]](#cite_note-gan.philliptsmall.me.uk-66)

### Independence and republic

Main article: [Independence of the Maldives](/wiki/Independence_of_the_Maldives "Independence of the Maldives")

When the British became increasingly unable to continue their colonial hold on Asia and were losing their colonies to the indigenous populations who wanted freedom, on 26 July 1965 an agreement was signed on behalf of the Sultan by Ibrahim Nasir Rannabandeyri Kilegefan, Prime Minister, and on behalf of the British government by [Sir Michael Walker](/wiki/Michael_Walker_(diplomat) "Michael Walker (diplomat)"), British Ambassador-designate to the Maldive Islands, which formally ended the British authority on the defence and external affairs of Maldives.[[63]](#cite_note-67) The islands thus achieved independence, with the ceremony taking place at the British High Commissioner's Residence in [Colombo](/wiki/Colombo "Colombo"). After this, the sultanate continued for another three years under [Sir Muhammad Fareed Didi](/wiki/Muhammad_Fareed_Didi "Muhammad Fareed Didi"), who declared himself King upon independence.[[64]](#cite_note-68)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Flag_of_the_Sultan_of_The_Maldives.svg/250px-Flag_of_the_Sultan_of_The_Maldives.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Flag_of_the_Sultan_of_The_Maldives.svg)

Flag of the [Sultan of Maldives](/wiki/List_of_Maldivian_monarchs "List of Maldivian monarchs")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Prime_Minister_Ibrahim_Nasir_signs_independence_papers.jpg/250px-Prime_Minister_Ibrahim_Nasir_signs_independence_papers.jpg)](/wiki/File:Prime_Minister_Ibrahim_Nasir_signs_independence_papers.jpg)

Prime Minister Ibrahim Nasir signs independence agreement with the British on July 26, 1965.

On 15 November 1967, a vote was taken in parliament to decide whether Maldives should continue as a constitutional monarchy or become a republic.[[65]](#cite_note-69) Of the 44 members of parliament, 40 voted in favour of a republic. On 15 March 1968, a [national referendum](/wiki/1968_Maldivian_constitutional_referendum "1968 Maldivian constitutional referendum") was held on the question, and 93.34% of those taking part voted in favour of establishing a republic.[[66]](#cite_note-70) The republic was declared on 11 November 1968, thus ending the 853-year-old monarchy, which was replaced by a republic under the presidency of Ibrahim Nasir.[[67]](#cite_note-71) As the King had held little real power, this was seen as a cosmetic change and required few alterations in the structures of government.

[Tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_Maldives "Tourism in the Maldives") began to be developed on the [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") by the beginning of the 1970s.[[68]](#cite_note-72) The first resort in Maldives was [Kurumba Maldives](/wiki/Kurumba_Maldives "Kurumba Maldives") which welcomed the first guests on 3 October 1972.[[69]](#cite_note-73) The first accurate census was held in December 1977 and showed 142,832 people living in Maldives.[[70]](#cite_note-74)

Political infighting during the 1970s between Nasir's faction and other political figures led to the 1975 arrest and exile of elected prime minister [Ahmed Zaki](/wiki/Ahmed_Zaki_(politician) "Ahmed Zaki (politician)") to a remote [atoll](/wiki/Atoll "Atoll").[[71]](#cite_note-75) Economic decline followed the closure of the [British airfield at Gan](/wiki/Gan_International_Airport "Gan International Airport") and the collapse of the market for dried fish, an important export. With support for his administration faltering, Nasir fled to Singapore in 1978, with millions of dollars from the treasury.[[72]](#cite_note-76)

[Maumoon Abdul Gayoom](/wiki/Maumoon_Abdul_Gayoom "Maumoon Abdul Gayoom") began his 30-year role as president in 1978, winning six consecutive elections without opposition. His election was seen as ushering in a period of political stability and economic development given Maumoon's priority to develop the poorer islands. Tourism flourished and increased foreign contact spurred development. However, Maumoon's rule was controversial, with some critics saying Maumoon was an autocrat who quelled dissent by limiting freedoms and practising political favouritism.[[73]](#cite_note-cnn_sinking-77)

A series of coup attempts (in 1980, 1983, and 1988) by Nasir supporters and business interests tried to topple the government without success. While the first two attempts met with little success, the [1988 coup attempt](/wiki/1988_Maldives_coup_attempt "1988 Maldives coup attempt") involved a roughly 80-strong mercenary force of the [PLOTE](/wiki/People%27s_Liberation_Organisation_of_Tamil_Eelam "People's Liberation Organisation of Tamil Eelam") who seized the airport and caused Maumoon to flee from house to house until the intervention of 1,600 [Indian troops](/wiki/Indian_Armed_Forces "Indian Armed Forces") airlifted into Malé restored order.

The November 1988 coup d'état was headed by Ibrahim Lutfee, a businessman, and Sikka Ahmed Ismail Manik, the father of the former first lady of Maldives [Fazna Ahmed](/wiki/Fazna_Ahmed "Fazna Ahmed").[[74]](#cite_note-78) The attackers were defeated by then [National Security Services of Maldives](/wiki/Maldives_National_Defence_Force "Maldives National Defence Force").[[75]](#cite_note-three-79) On the night of 3 November 1988, the [Indian Air Force](/wiki/Indian_Air_Force "Indian Air Force") airlifted a [parachute battalion group](/wiki/Parachute_Regiment_(India)#Operation_Cactus "Parachute Regiment (India)") from [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra") and flew them over 2,000 kilometres (1,200 mi) to Maldives.[[75]](#cite_note-three-79) By the time Indian armed forces reached Maldives, the mercenary forces has already left Malé on the hijacked ship MV Progress Light.[[75]](#cite_note-three-79) The Indian [paratroopers](/wiki/Paratroopers "Paratroopers") landed at [Hulhulé](/wiki/Hulhul%C3%A9_Island "Hulhulé Island") and secured the airfield and restored the government rule at Malé within hours.[[75]](#cite_note-three-79) The brief operation labelled *Operation Cactus*, also involved the [Indian Navy](/wiki/Indian_Navy "Indian Navy") that assisted in capturing the freighter MV Progress Light and rescued the hostages and crew.[[75]](#cite_note-three-79)

### 21st century

Main article: [History of the Maldives § 21st century](/wiki/History_of_the_Maldives#21st_century "History of the Maldives")

Maldives [were devastated](/wiki/Effect_of_the_2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_on_the_Maldives "Effect of the 2004 Indian Ocean earthquake on the Maldives") by a [tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake_and_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami") on 26 December 2004, following the [Indian Ocean earthquake](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_earthquake "2004 Indian Ocean earthquake"). Only nine islands were reported to have escaped any flooding,[[76]](#cite_note-80)[[77]](#cite_note-81) while fifty-seven islands faced serious damage to critical infrastructure, fourteen islands had to be totally evacuated, and six islands were destroyed. A further twenty-one resort islands were forced to close because of tsunami damage. The total damage was estimated at more than US$400 million, or some 62% of the GDP.[[78]](#cite_note-82) 102 Maldivians and 6 foreigners reportedly died in the tsunami.[[73]](#cite_note-cnn_sinking-77) The destructive impact of the waves on the low-lying islands was mitigated by the fact there was no continental shelf or land mass upon which the waves could gain height. The tallest waves were reported to be 14 feet (4.3 m) high.[[79]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/The_Prime_Minister%2C_Dr._Manmohan_Singh_along_with_other_Head_of_State_and_Government_of_SAARC_Countries%2C_at_the_inaugural_session_of_the_17th_SAARC_Summit%2C_at_Adu_Atoll_in_Maldives_on_November_10%2C_2011.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Prime_Minister,_Dr._Manmohan_Singh_along_with_other_Head_of_State_and_Government_of_SAARC_Countries,_at_the_inaugural_session_of_the_17th_SAARC_Summit,_at_Adu_Atoll_in_Maldives_on_November_10,_2011.jpg)

[17th SAARC summit](/wiki/17th_SAARC_summit "17th SAARC summit") in [Addu City](/wiki/Addu_City "Addu City"), Maldives

During the later part of Maumoon's rule, independent political movements emerged in Maldives, which challenged the then-ruling [Dhivehi Rayyithunge Party](/wiki/Dhivehi_Rayyithunge_Party "Dhivehi Rayyithunge Party") (Maldivian People's Party, MPP) and demanded democratic reform. The dissident journalist and activist [Mohamed Nasheed](/wiki/Mohamed_Nasheed "Mohamed Nasheed") founded the [Maldivian Democratic Party](/wiki/Maldivian_Democratic_Party "Maldivian Democratic Party") (MDP) in 2003 and pressured Maumoon into allowing gradual political reforms.[[80]](#cite_note-brownpoliticalreview.org-84) In 2008, a new constitution was approved and the [first direct presidential elections](/wiki/2008_Maldivian_presidential_election "2008 Maldivian presidential election") occurred, which were won by Nasheed in the second round. His administration faced many challenges, including the huge debt left by the previous government, the economic downturn following the 2004 tsunami, overspending using overprinting of local currency (the [rufiyaa](/wiki/Rufiyaa "Rufiyaa")), unemployment, corruption, and increasing drug use.[[81]](#cite_note-85)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] Taxation on goods was imposed for the first time in the country, and import duties were reduced on many goods and services. Universal health insurance ([Aasandha](/wiki/Aasandha "Aasandha")) and social welfare benefits were given to those aged 65 years or older, single parents, and those with special needs.[[73]](#cite_note-cnn_sinking-77)

Social and [political unrest grew in late 2011](/wiki/2011%E2%80%932013_Maldives_political_crisis "2011–2013 Maldives political crisis"), following opposition campaigns in the name of protecting Islam. Nasheed controversially resigned from office after a large number of police and army mutinied in February 2012. Nasheed's vice-president, [Mohamed Waheed Hassan Manik](/wiki/Mohamed_Waheed_Hassan "Mohamed Waheed Hassan"), was sworn in as president.[[82]](#cite_note-86)
Nasheed was later arrested,[[83]](#cite_note-87) convicted of terrorism, and sentenced to 13 years. The trial was widely seen as flawed and political. The [UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention](/wiki/Working_Group_on_Arbitrary_Detention "Working Group on Arbitrary Detention") called for Nasheed's immediate release.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

The [2013 Maldivian presidential election](/wiki/2013_Maldivian_presidential_election "2013 Maldivian presidential election") results were highly contested. Former president Nasheed won the most votes in the first round, but the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_Maldives "Supreme Court of the Maldives") annulled it despite the positive assessment of international election observers. In the re-run vote [Abdulla Yameen](/wiki/Abdulla_Yameen "Abdulla Yameen"), the half-brother of the former president Maumoon, assumed the presidency.[[80]](#cite_note-brownpoliticalreview.org-84) Yameen survived an assassination attempt in late 2015.[[85]](#cite_note-89) Vice-president [Mohamed Jameel Ahmed](/wiki/Mohamed_Jameel_Ahmed "Mohamed Jameel Ahmed") was removed from office after a [no confidence motion](/wiki/No_confidence_motion "No confidence motion") from the People's Majlis, it was alleged that he was conspiring with opposition political parties and planning riots.[[86]](#cite_note-90) Vice-president [Ahmed Adeeb](/wiki/Ahmed_Adeeb "Ahmed Adeeb") was later arrested together with 17 supporters for "public order offences" and the government instituted a broader crackdown against his accomplices. A [state of emergency](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency") was later declared ahead of a planned anti-government rally,[[87]](#cite_note-91) and the People's Majlis (parliament) accelerated the removal of Adeeb.[[88]](#cite_note-majlis.gov.mv-92)[[89]](#cite_note-93)

[In the 2018 election](/wiki/2018_Maldivian_presidential_election "2018 Maldivian presidential election"), [Ibrahim Mohamed Solih](/wiki/Ibrahim_Mohamed_Solih "Ibrahim Mohamed Solih") won the most votes, and was sworn in as Maldives' new president in November 2018. Adeeb was freed by courts in Male in July 2019 after his conviction on charges of terrorism and corruption was overruled but was placed under a [travel ban](/wiki/Travel_ban "Travel ban") after the state prosecutor appealed the order in a corruption and money laundering case. Adeeb escaped in a [tugboat](/wiki/Tugboat "Tugboat") to [seek asylum](/wiki/Asylum_seeker "Asylum seeker") in India. It is understood that the [Indian Coast Guard](/wiki/Indian_Coast_Guard "Indian Coast Guard") escorted the tugboat to the International Maritime Boundary Line (IMBL) and he was then "transferred" to a [Maldivian Coast Guard](/wiki/Maldivian_Coast_Guard "Maldivian Coast Guard") ship, where officials took him into custody.[[90]](#cite_note-94)
Former president Abdulla Yameen was sentenced to five years in prison in November 2019 for money laundering. The [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_the_Maldives "High Court of the Maldives") upheld the jail sentence in January 2021.[[91]](#cite_note-95) However, Supreme Court overturned Yameen's conviction in November 2021.[[92]](#cite_note-96)

In the [2023 election](/wiki/2023_Maldivian_presidential_election "2023 Maldivian presidential election"), [People's National Congress](/wiki/People%27s_National_Congress_(Maldives) "People's National Congress (Maldives)") (PNC) candidate [Mohamed Muizzu](/wiki/Mohamed_Muizzu "Mohamed Muizzu") won the second-round runoff of Maldives presidential election, beating incumbent president, Ibrahim Solih, with 54% of the vote.[[93]](#cite_note-97) On 17 October 2023, Mohamed Muizzu was sworn in as the eighth President of the Republic of Maldives.[[94]](#cite_note-98) Mohamed Muizzu is widely seen to be pro-China, souring relations with India.[[95]](#cite_note-99) In 2024, ex-President Abdulla Yameen Abdul Gayoom was freed from his 11-year conviction and the High Court ordered a new trial.[[96]](#cite_note-100)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of the Maldives](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Maldives "Geography of the Maldives")

Maldives consists of 1,192 [coral islands](/wiki/Coral_island "Coral island") grouped in a double chain of 26 atolls, that stretch along a length of 871 kilometres (541 miles) north to south, 130 kilometres (81 miles) east to west, spread over roughly 90,000 square kilometres (35,000 sq mi), of which only 298 km2 (115 sq mi) is dry land, making this one of the world's most dispersed countries. It lies between latitudes [1°S](/wiki/1st_parallel_south "1st parallel south") and [8°N](/wiki/8th_parallel_north "8th parallel north"), and longitudes [72°](/wiki/72nd_meridian_east "72nd meridian east") and [74°E](/wiki/74th_meridian_east "74th meridian east"). The atolls are composed of live [coral reefs](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef") and [sand bars](/wiki/Sand_bar "Sand bar"), situated atop a submarine ridge 960 kilometres (600 mi) long that rises abruptly from the depths of the Indian Ocean and runs north to south.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Malosmadulu_Atolls%2C_Maldives.jpg/250px-Malosmadulu_Atolls%2C_Maldives.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malosmadulu_Atolls,_Maldives.jpg)

Maalhosmadulu Atoll seen from space. [Northern Maalhosmadulu Atoll](/wiki/Northern_Maalhosmadulu_Atoll "Northern Maalhosmadulu Atoll") and [Southern Maalhosmadulu Atoll](/wiki/Southern_Maalhosmadulu_Atoll "Southern Maalhosmadulu Atoll") can be seen in this picture.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Man_on_sand_bank_in_the_Maldives_looking_at_sea.jpg/250px-Man_on_sand_bank_in_the_Maldives_looking_at_sea.jpg)](/wiki/File:Man_on_sand_bank_in_the_Maldives_looking_at_sea.jpg)

Dark clouds bringing heavy rain, common in the rainy season

Only near the southern end of this natural coral barricade do two open passages permit safe ship navigation from one side of the Indian Ocean to the other through the territorial waters of Maldives. For administrative purposes, the Maldivian government organised these atolls into 21 [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_Maldives "Administrative divisions of the Maldives"). The largest island of Maldives is that of [Gan](/wiki/Gan_(Laamu_Atoll) "Gan (Laamu Atoll)"), which belongs to Laamu Atoll or Hahdhummathi Maldives. In Addu Atoll, the westernmost islands are connected by roads over the reef (collectively called Link Road), and the total length of the road is 14 km (9 mi).

Maldives is the lowest country in the world, with maximum and average natural ground levels of only 2.4 metres (7 ft 10 in) and 1.5 metres (4 ft 11 in) above sea level, respectively. In areas where construction exists, however, this has been increased to several metres. More than 80 per cent of the country's land is composed of coral islands which rise less than one metre above sea level.[[97]](#cite_note-101) As a result, Maldives are in danger of being submerged due to [rising sea levels](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"). The [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change") has warned that, at current rates, sea-level rise would be high enough to make Maldives uninhabitable by 2100.[[98]](#cite_note-102)[[99]](#cite_note-103)

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Bathala_%28Maldives%29_8.JPG/250px-Bathala_%28Maldives%29_8.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bathala_(Maldives)_8.JPG)

A beach of [Bathala](/wiki/Bathala_(island) "Bathala (island)") island

Maldives has a [tropical monsoon climate](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate") (Am) under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), which is affected by the large landmass of South Asia to the north. Because Maldives has the lowest elevation of any country in the world, the temperature is constantly hot and often humid. The presence of this landmass causes differential heating of land and water. These factors set off a rush of moisture-rich air from the Indian Ocean over South Asia, resulting in the southwest [monsoon](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon"). Two seasons dominate Maldives' weather: the dry season associated with the winter northeastern monsoon and the rainy season associated with the southwest monsoon which brings strong winds and storms.[[100]](#cite_note-Climate-104)

The shift from the dry northeast monsoon to the moist southwest monsoon occurs during April and May. During this period, the southwest winds contribute to the formation of the southwest monsoon, which reaches Maldives at the beginning of June and lasts until the end of November. However, the weather patterns of Maldives do not always conform to the monsoon patterns of South Asia. The annual rainfall averages 254 centimetres (100 in) in the north and 381 centimetres (150 in) in the south.[[101]](#cite_note-105)[[100]](#cite_note-Climate-104)

The monsoonal influence is greater in the north of Maldives than in the south, more influenced by the [equatorial currents](/wiki/Equatorial_Counter_Current "Equatorial Counter Current").

The average high temperature is 31.5 degrees Celsius and the average low temperature is 26.4 degrees Celsius.[[100]](#cite_note-Climate-104)
