### Sea level rise

Main article: [Climate change in the Maldives](/wiki/Climate_change_in_the_Maldives "Climate change in the Maldives")

See also: [Effects of climate change on island nations](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change_on_island_nations "Effects of climate change on island nations") and [The Island President](/wiki/The_Island_President "The Island President")

In 1988, Maldivian authorities claimed that sea rise would "completely cover this Indian Ocean nation of 1,196 small islands within the next 30 years."[[104]](#cite_note-108)

The [Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change](/wiki/Intergovernmental_Panel_on_Climate_Change "Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change")'s 2007 report predicted the upper limit of the [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") will be 59 centimetres (23 in) by 2100, which means that most of the republic's 200 inhabited islands may need to be abandoned.[[105]](#cite_note-109) According to researchers from the [University of Southampton](/wiki/University_of_Southampton "University of Southampton"), Maldives are the third most endangered island nation due to flooding from [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") as a percentage of population.[[106]](#cite_note-110)

In 2008, Nasheed announced plans to look into purchasing new land in India, Sri Lanka, and Australia because he was concerned about global warming, and the possibility of much of the islands being inundated with water from rising sea levels. The purchase of land will be made from a fund generated by tourism. The president explained his intentions: "We do not want to leave Maldives, but we also do not want to be [climate refugees](/wiki/Climate_migration "Climate migration") living in tents for decades".[[107]](#cite_note-Guardian-111)

At the 2009 International Climate Talks, Nasheed stated that:

> For us swearing off fossil fuels is not only the right thing to do, but it is also in our economic self-interest... Pioneering countries will free themselves from the unpredictable price of foreign oil; they will capitalise on the new green economy of the future, and they will enhance their moral standing giving them greater political influence on the world stage.[[108]](#cite_note-112)

Former president [Mohamed Nasheed](/wiki/Mohamed_Nasheed "Mohamed Nasheed") said in 2012 "If carbon emissions continue at the rate they are climbing today, my country will be underwater in seven years."[[109]](#cite_note-Guilfordian-113) He has called for more [climate change mitigation](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") action while on the American television shows *[The Daily Show](/wiki/The_Daily_Show "The Daily Show")*[[110]](#cite_note-114) and the *[Late Show with David Letterman](/wiki/Late_Show_with_David_Letterman "Late Show with David Letterman")*,[[109]](#cite_note-Guilfordian-113) and hosted "the world's first underwater cabinet meeting" in 2009 to raise awareness of the threats posed by climate change.[[111]](#cite_note-115)[[112]](#cite_note-:1-116) Concerns over rising sea levels have also been expressed by Nasheed's predecessor, [Maumoon Abdul Gayoom](/wiki/Maumoon_Abdul_Gayoom "Maumoon Abdul Gayoom").[[113]](#cite_note-117)

In 2020, a three-year study at the [University of Plymouth](/wiki/University_of_Plymouth "University of Plymouth") which looked at Maldives and the [Marshall Islands](/wiki/Marshall_Islands "Marshall Islands"), found that tides move sediment to create a higher elevation, a morphological response that the researchers suggested could help low-lying islands adjust to sea level rise and keep the islands habitable. The research also reported that [sea walls](/wiki/Seawall "Seawall") were compromising islands' ability to adjust to rising sea levels and that island drowning is an inevitable outcome for islands with coastal structures like sea walls.[[114]](#cite_note-118) Hideki Kanamaru, natural resources officer with the [Food and Agriculture Organization](/wiki/Food_and_Agriculture_Organization "Food and Agriculture Organization") in Asia-Pacific, said the study provided a "new perspective" on how island nations could tackle the challenge of sea-level rise, and that even if islands can adapt naturally to higher seas by raising their own crests, humans still needed to double down on global warming and protection for island populations.[[112]](#cite_note-:1-116)

### Environment

Environmental issues other than [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") include bad waste disposal and [sand theft](/wiki/Sand_theft "Sand theft"). Although Maldives are kept relatively pristine and little [litter](/wiki/Litter "Litter") can be found on the islands, most [waste disposal](/wiki/Waste_management "Waste management") sites are often substandard. The bulk of the waste from Malé and nearby resorts in Maldives is disposed of at [Thilafushi](/wiki/Thilafushi "Thilafushi"), an industrial island on top of a lagoon reclaimed in the early '90s to sort waste management issues which had plagued the capital and surrounding islands.[[115]](#cite_note-119)

[31 protected areas](/wiki/List_of_Protected_Areas_of_Maldives "List of Protected Areas of Maldives") are administered by the Ministry of Climate Change, Environment and Energy and the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) of Maldives.[[116]](#cite_note-Maldives_Protected_Areas-120)

### Marine ecosystem

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Maldives_soft_coral.jpg/250px-Maldives_soft_coral.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maldives_soft_coral.jpg)

Maldives soft coral

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Maldives_Oriental_sweetlips%2C_Plectorhinchus_vittatus.jpg/250px-Maldives_Oriental_sweetlips%2C_Plectorhinchus_vittatus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maldives_Oriental_sweetlips,_Plectorhinchus_vittatus.jpg)

[Oriental sweetlips](/wiki/Oriental_sweetlips "Oriental sweetlips") (*Plectorhinchus vittatus*) at [Meeru Island](/wiki/Meeru_Island "Meeru Island"), North Male Atoll

The waters surrounding Maldives contain diverse marine life, including various [corals](/wiki/Coral "Coral"), over 2,000 species of fish,[[117]](#cite_note-121) and rare species of biological and commercial significance, with [tuna fisheries](/wiki/Tuna_fisheries "Tuna fisheries") representing a longstanding traditional resource. Freshwater fish such as the [milkfish](/wiki/Milkfish "Milkfish") ([Chanos chanos](/wiki/Milkfish "Milkfish")) and various smaller species thrive within ponds, marshes and other limited freshwater habitats. In the 1970s, a [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") agency[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] facilitated the introduction of the [tilapia](/wiki/Tilapia "Tilapia") or mouth-breeder.

Maldives have different habitats including [deep sea](/wiki/Deep_sea "Deep sea"), shallow coast, and reef ecosystems, fringing [mangroves](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove"), wetlands, and dry land. 187 species of coral form the [coral reefs](/wiki/Coral_reefs "Coral reefs"). This area of the Indian Ocean, alone, houses 1,100 [species](/wiki/Species "Species") of fish, 5 species of [sea turtle](/wiki/Sea_turtle "Sea turtle"), 21 species of whale and dolphin, 400 species of [mollusc](/wiki/Mollusc "Mollusc"), and 83 species of [echinoderms](/wiki/Echinoderms "Echinoderms"). The area is also populated by several crustacean species: 120 [copepods](/wiki/Copepod "Copepod"), and 15 [amphipods](/wiki/Amphipod "Amphipod"), as well as more than 145 [crab](/wiki/Crab "Crab") and 48 [shrimp](/wiki/Shrimp "Shrimp") species.[[118]](#cite_note-122)

Among the many marine families represented are [pufferfish](/wiki/Pufferfish "Pufferfish"), [fusiliers](/wiki/Caesionidae "Caesionidae"), [jackfish](/wiki/Carangidae "Carangidae"), [lionfish](/wiki/Lionfish "Lionfish"), [oriental sweetlips](/wiki/Oriental_sweetlips "Oriental sweetlips"), [reef sharks](/wiki/Reef_sharks "Reef sharks"), [groupers](/wiki/Grouper "Grouper"), eels, [snappers](/wiki/Lutjanidae "Lutjanidae"), [bannerfish](/wiki/Bannerfish "Bannerfish"), [batfish](/wiki/Ogcocephalidae "Ogcocephalidae"), [humphead wrasse](/wiki/Humphead_wrasse "Humphead wrasse"), [spotted eagle rays](/wiki/Spotted_eagle_ray "Spotted eagle ray"), [scorpionfish](/wiki/Scorpionfish "Scorpionfish"), [lobsters](/wiki/Lobster "Lobster"), [nudibranches](/wiki/Nudibranches "Nudibranches"), [angelfish](/wiki/Pomacanthidae "Pomacanthidae"), [butterflyfish](/wiki/Butterflyfish "Butterflyfish"), [squirrelfish](/wiki/Squirrelfish "Squirrelfish"), [soldierfish](/wiki/Soldierfish "Soldierfish"), [glassfish](/wiki/Asiatic_glassfish "Asiatic glassfish"), [surgeonfish](/wiki/Surgeonfish "Surgeonfish"), [unicornfish](/wiki/Naso_(fish) "Naso (fish)"), [triggerfish](/wiki/Triggerfish "Triggerfish"), [Napoleon wrasse](/wiki/Napoleon_wrasse "Napoleon wrasse"), and [barracuda](/wiki/Barracuda "Barracuda").[[119]](#cite_note-scuba-123)[[120]](#cite_note-124)

These coral reefs are home to various marine ecosystems that include species such as [planktonic](/wiki/Planktonic "Planktonic") organisms and [whale sharks](/wiki/Whale_shark "Whale shark"). Five species of [sponge](/wiki/Sponge "Sponge") have displayed anti-[tumor](/wiki/Tumor "Tumor") and anti-[cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") properties.[[121]](#cite_note-Coral_reefs-125)

In 1998, sea-temperature warming of as much as 5 °C (9.0 °F) due to a single [El Niño phenomenon](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o-Southern_Oscillation "El Niño-Southern Oscillation") event caused [coral bleaching](/wiki/Coral_bleaching "Coral bleaching") and killed two-thirds of the nation's coral reefs.[[122]](#cite_note-Wheatley-126)

To induce the regrowth of the reefs, scientists placed electrified cones anywhere from 20–60 feet (6.1–18.3 m) below the surface to provide a substrate for larval coral attachment. In 2004, scientists witnessed corals regenerating. Corals began to eject pink-orange eggs and sperm. The growth of these electrified corals was five times faster than untreated corals.[[122]](#cite_note-Wheatley-126) Scientist Azeez Hakim stated:

> Before 1998, we never thought that this reef would die. We had always taken for granted that these animals would be there, that this reef would be there forever. El Niño gave us a wake-up call that these things are not going to be there forever. Not only this, but they also act as a natural barrier against tropical storms, floods, and tsunamis. Seaweeds grow on the skeletons of dead coral.

— [[119]](#cite_note-scuba-123)

Again, in 2016, the coral reefs of Maldives experienced a [severe bleaching incident](/wiki/Coral_bleaching#Maldives "Coral bleaching"). Up to 95% of coral around some islands have died, and, even after six months, 100% of young coral transplants died. The surface water temperatures reached an all-time high in 2016, at 31 degrees Celsius in May.[[123]](#cite_note-127)

Recent scientific studies suggest that the faunistic composition can vary greatly between neighbour atolls, especially in terms of [benthic fauna](/wiki/Benthic_fauna "Benthic fauna"). Differences in terms of fishing pressure (including [poaching](/wiki/Poaching "Poaching")) could be the cause.[[124]](#cite_note-Ducarme_between-128)

### Wildlife

Main article: [Wildlife of the Maldives](/wiki/Wildlife_of_the_Maldives "Wildlife of the Maldives")

[![Sharks](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Rekiny_w%C4%85sate%2C_Malediwy.jpg/250px-Rekiny_w%C4%85sate%2C_Malediwy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rekiny_w%C4%85sate,_Malediwy.jpg)

[![Nature Park](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Addu_City%2C_Maldives_%28Unsplash_UWCIzF2gkdg%29.jpg/250px-Addu_City%2C_Maldives_%28Unsplash_UWCIzF2gkdg%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Addu_City,_Maldives_(Unsplash_UWCIzF2gkdg).jpg)

[![Flower](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Maldives_09642.JPG/250px-Maldives_09642.JPG)](/wiki/File:Maldives_09642.JPG)

[![Bird](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Maldives_00345.JPG/250px-Maldives_00345.JPG)](/wiki/File:Maldives_00345.JPG)

Clockwise from top left: [Tawny nurse sharks](/wiki/Tawny_nurse_shark "Tawny nurse shark") near [Vaavu Atoll](/wiki/Vaavu_Atoll "Vaavu Atoll"), pier in [Addu City](/wiki/Addu_City "Addu City"), *[Butorides striata](/wiki/Butorides_striata "Butorides striata")*, and *[Ixora](/wiki/Ixora "Ixora") sp.*

The [wildlife of Maldives](/wiki/Wildlife_of_the_Maldives "Wildlife of the Maldives") includes the [flora](/wiki/Flora "Flora") and [fauna](/wiki/Fauna "Fauna") of the [islands](/wiki/Islands_of_the_Maldives "Islands of the Maldives"), reefs, and the surrounding ocean. Recent scientific studies suggest that the fauna varies greatly between [atolls](/wiki/Atolls_of_the_Maldives "Atolls of the Maldives") following a north–south gradient, but important differences between neighbouring atolls were also found (especially in terms of sea animals), which may be linked to differences in fishing pressure – including poaching.[[125]](#cite_note-Ducarme-2016-V36-129)

The terrestrial habitats of Maldives are confronted with a significant threat as extensive development encroaches swiftly upon the limited land resources. Once seldom frequented, previously uninhabited islands now teeter on the brink of extinction, virtually devoid of untouched expanses. Over recent decades of intensive development, numerous natural environments crucial to indigenous species have suffered severe endangerment or outright destruction.

Coral reef habitats have been damaged, as the pressure for land has brought about the creation of artificial islands. Some reefs have been filled with rubble with little regard for the changes in the currents on the reef shelf and how the new pattern would affect coral growth and its related life forms on the reef edges.[[126]](#cite_note-130) Mangroves thrive in brackish or muddy regions of Maldives. The archipelago hosts fourteen species spanning ten genera, among which is the fern Acrostichum aureum, indigenous to these islands.[[127]](#cite_note-Hingun-2015-131)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Maldives_00246.JPG/250px-Maldives_00246.JPG)](/wiki/File:Maldives_00246.JPG)

*[Butorides striata](/wiki/Butorides_striata "Butorides striata")* in Maldives

Due to their diminutive size, land-dwelling reptiles are scarce on the Maldivian islands. Among the limited terrestrial reptilian inhabitants are a species of [gecko](/wiki/Gecko "Gecko") and the oriental garden lizard ([Calotes versicolor](/wiki/Calotes_versicolor "Calotes versicolor")), alongside the white-spotted supple skink ([Riopa albopunctata](/wiki/Riopa_albopunctata "Riopa albopunctata")), the Indian wolf snake ([Lycodon aulicus](/wiki/Lycodon_aulicus "Lycodon aulicus")), and the Brahmin blind snake ([Ramphotyphlops braminus](/wiki/Ramphotyphlops_braminus "Ramphotyphlops braminus")).

In the surrounding seas, however, a more diverse array of reptilian life thrives. Maldivian beaches serve as nesting grounds for the green sea turtle ([Chelonia mydas](/wiki/Chelonia_mydas "Chelonia mydas")), the [hawksbill sea turtle](/wiki/Hawksbill_sea_turtle "Hawksbill sea turtle"), and the [leatherback sea turtle](/wiki/Leatherback_sea_turtle "Leatherback sea turtle"). Furthermore, [saltwater crocodiles](/wiki/Saltwater_crocodiles "Saltwater crocodiles") have been reported to occasionally reach the islands, taking residence in marshy regions.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

The location of this Indian Ocean archipelago means that its avifauna is mainly restricted to [pelagic birds](/wiki/Pelagic_bird "Pelagic bird").[[129]](#cite_note-133) Most of the species are Eurasian migratory birds, only a few being typically associated with the Indian sub-continent. Some, like the [frigatebird](/wiki/Frigatebird "Frigatebird"), are seasonal. Some birds dwell in marshes and island bush, like the [grey heron](/wiki/Grey_heron "Grey heron") and the [moorhen](/wiki/Moorhen "Moorhen"). [White terns](/wiki/White_terns "White terns") are found occasionally on the southern islands due to their rich habitats.[[130]](#cite_note-134)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of the Maldives](/wiki/Politics_of_the_Maldives "Politics of the Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Dr_Muizzu_in_December_2023.jpg/250px-Dr_Muizzu_in_December_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dr_Muizzu_in_December_2023.jpg)

[Mohamed Muizzu](/wiki/Mohamed_Muizzu "Mohamed Muizzu"), [President](/wiki/President_of_the_Maldives "President of the Maldives") since 2023

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Hussain_Mohamed_Latheef_official_portrait_January_2024.jpg/250px-Hussain_Mohamed_Latheef_official_portrait_January_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hussain_Mohamed_Latheef_official_portrait_January_2024.jpg)

[Hussain Mohamed Latheef](/wiki/Hussain_Mohamed_Latheef "Hussain Mohamed Latheef"), [Vice President](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_Maldives "Vice President of the Maldives") since 2023

Maldives is a [presidential](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system") [constitutional republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic"), with extensive influence of the president as [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") and [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state"). The president heads the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") and appoints the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_Maldives "Cabinet of the Maldives") which is approved by the [People's Majlis (Parliament)](/wiki/Majlis_of_the_Maldives "Majlis of the Maldives"). He leads the [armed forces](/wiki/Maldives_National_Defence_Force "Maldives National Defence Force"). The current president serving since 17 November 2023 is [Mohamed Muizzu](/wiki/Mohamed_Muizzu "Mohamed Muizzu").[[131]](#cite_note-135)[[132]](#cite_note-136) [President of Maldives](/wiki/President_of_the_Maldives "President of the Maldives") and Members of the [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") Majlis serve five-year terms.[[133]](#cite_note-137) The total number of members are determined by atoll populations. At the [2024 parliamentary election](/wiki/2024_Maldivian_parliamentary_election "2024 Maldivian parliamentary election"), the [People's National Congress](/wiki/People%27s_National_Congress_(Maldives) "People's National Congress (Maldives)") (PNC) won a super-majority over the 93 constituencies.[[134]](#cite_note-138)

The republican constitution came into force in 1968 and was amended in 1970, 1972, and 1975.[[135]](#cite_note-139) On 27 November 1997 it was replaced by another Constitution assented to by then-President [Maumoon](/wiki/Maumoon_Abdul_Gayoom "Maumoon Abdul Gayoom"). This Constitution came into force on 1 January 1998.[[136]](#cite_note-140) The current [Constitution of Maldives](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Maldives "Constitution of the Maldives") was ratified by President Maumoon on 7 August 2008, and came into effect immediately, replacing and repealing the constitution of 1998. This new constitution includes a judiciary run by an independent commission, and independent commissions to oversee elections and fight corruption. It also reduces the executive powers vested under the president and strengthens the parliament. All state that the [president](/wiki/President_of_the_Maldives "President of the Maldives") is head of state, head of government, and Commander-in-Chief of the [armed forces](/wiki/Maldives_National_Defence_Force "Maldives National Defence Force") of Maldives.

In 2018, the then ruling [Progressive Party of Maldives](/wiki/Progressive_Party_of_Maldives "Progressive Party of Maldives") (PPM)'s tensions with opposition parties and the subsequent crackdown was termed as an "assault on democracy" by the [UN Human Rights](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Council "United Nations Human Rights Council") chief.[[137]](#cite_note-141)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/President_congratulates_the_new_Speaker_of_the_People%E2%80%99s_Majlis.jpg/250px-President_congratulates_the_new_Speaker_of_the_People%E2%80%99s_Majlis.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_congratulates_the_new_Speaker_of_the_People%E2%80%99s_Majlis.jpg)

President Ibrahim Mohamed Solih congratulates [Mohamed Nasheed](/wiki/Mohamed_Nasheed "Mohamed Nasheed") Speaker of the People's Majlis in May 2019

In the April 2019 parliamentary [election](/wiki/2019_Maldivian_parliamentary_election "2019 Maldivian parliamentary election") The [Maldivian Democratic Party](/wiki/Maldivian_Democratic_Party "Maldivian Democratic Party") (MDP) of President Ibrahim Mohamed Solih won a landslide victory. It took 65 of 87 seats of the parliament.[[138]](#cite_note-142) This was the first time a single party was able to get such a high number of seats in the parliament in Maldivian history.[[139]](#cite_note-143)

[Order of Nishanizzuddeen](/wiki/Order_of_the_Distinguished_Rule_of_Izzuddin "Order of the Distinguished Rule of Izzuddin") is Maldives' highest civilian honour that can be bestowed upon a person. It is awarded by the president, usually in an elaborate ceremony.[[140]](#cite_note-144)

In April 2024, President Mohamed Muizzu's pro-China People's National Congress (PNC) won 66 seats in the 2024 [Maldivian parliamentary election](/wiki/Maldivian_parliamentary_election,_2024 "Maldivian parliamentary election, 2024"), while its allies took nine, giving the president the backing of 75 legislators in the 93-member house, meaning a super-majority and enough to change the constitution.[[141]](#cite_note-145)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of the Maldives](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_Maldives "Administrative divisions of the Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Atolls_of_the_maldives.png/250px-Atolls_of_the_maldives.png)](/wiki/File:Atolls_of_the_maldives.png)

Each administrative atoll is marked, along with the [thaana](/wiki/Thaana "Thaana") letter used to identify the atoll. Natural atolls are labelled in light blue.

Maldives has twenty-six natural [atolls](/wiki/Atolls_of_the_Maldives "Atolls of the Maldives") and few island groups on isolated reefs, all of which have been divided into twenty-one administrative divisions (17 administrative atolls and cities of Malé, Addu, [Fuvahmulah](/wiki/Fuvahmulah "Fuvahmulah"), [Thinadhoo](/wiki/Thinadhoo_(Huvadhu_Atoll) "Thinadhoo (Huvadhu Atoll)"), and [Kulhudhuffushi](/wiki/Kulhudhuffushi "Kulhudhuffushi")).[[142]](#cite_note-146)

Each atoll is administered by an elected Atoll Council. The islands are administered by an elected Island Council.

In addition to a name, every administrative division is identified by the Maldivian code letters, such as "[Haa Alif](/wiki/Haa_Alif "Haa Alif")" for [Thiladhunmati Uthuruburi](/wiki/Northern_Thiladhunmathi_Atoll "Northern Thiladhunmathi Atoll") (Thiladhunmathi North); and by a Latin code letter. The first corresponds to the geographical Maldivian name of the atoll; the second is a code adopted for convenience. As certain islands in different atolls have the same name, for administrative purposes this code is quoted before the name of the island, for example: Baa Funadhoo, Kaafu Funadhoo, Gaafu-Alifu Funadhoo. Since most atolls have very long geographical names it is also used whenever the long name is inconvenient, for example in the atoll website names.[[143]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-147)

The introduction of code-letter names has been a source of much puzzlement and misunderstanding, especially among foreigners. Many people have come to think that the code letter of the administrative atoll is its new name and that it has replaced its geographical name. Under such circumstances, it is hard to know which is the correct name to use.[[143]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-147)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of the Maldives](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_the_Maldives "Foreign relations of the Maldives")

Since 1996, Maldives has been the official progress monitor of the [Indian Ocean Commission](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_Commission "Indian Ocean Commission"). In 2002, Maldives began to express interest in the commission but as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maldives&action=edit) had not applied for membership. Maldives' interest relates to its identity as a small island state, especially economic development and environmental preservation, and its desire for closer relations with France, a main actor in the IOC region.

Maldives is a founding member of the [South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation") ([SAARC](/wiki/South_Asian_Association_for_Regional_Cooperation "South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation")). The republic joined the Commonwealth in 1982, some 17 years after gaining independence from the United Kingdom. In October 2016, Maldives announced its withdrawal from the Commonwealth[[144]](#cite_note-148) in protest at allegations of human rights abuse and failing democracy.[[145]](#cite_note-149) Maldives enjoys close ties with Commonwealth members [Seychelles](/wiki/Seychelles "Seychelles") and [Mauritius](/wiki/Mauritius "Mauritius"). Maldives and [Comoros](/wiki/Comoros "Comoros") are also both members of the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation"). Following his election as president in 2018, Ibrahim Mohamed Solih's administration sought to rejoin the Commonwealth after showing evidence of democratic reform,[[146]](#cite_note-150) leading to its readmission on 1 February 2020.[[147]](#cite_note-151)

As a result of sanctions imposed upon the [Russian oligarchs](/wiki/Russian_oligarchs "Russian oligarchs") by the West in response to the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), many of them sought refuge for their mega-yachts in Maldives due to the absence of an extradition treaty with the United States and other countries.[[148]](#cite_note-152)

Following a cabinet meeting, in June 2024, the government of Maldives decided to ban [Israeli passport](/wiki/Israeli_passport "Israeli passport") holders from entering the country, as a response to the ongoing [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war").[[149]](#cite_note-153)[[150]](#cite_note-154)[[151]](#cite_note-155)

### Military

Main article: [Maldives National Defence Force](/wiki/Maldives_National_Defence_Force "Maldives National Defence Force")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Presidential_Address_2024_in_Maldives_%2849%29.jpg/250px-Presidential_Address_2024_in_Maldives_%2849%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presidential_Address_2024_in_Maldives_(49).jpg)

Maldivian soldiers at a presidential address in February 2024

Maldives National Defence Force is the combined security organisation responsible for defending the security and sovereignty of Maldives, having the primary task of being responsible for attending to all internal and external security needs of Maldives, including the protection of the [Exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone") (EEZ) and the maintenance of peace and security.[[152]](#cite_note-156) The MNDF component branches are the [Coast Guard](/wiki/Maldivian_Coast_Guard "Maldivian Coast Guard"), [Marine Corps](/wiki/MNDF_Marine_Corps "MNDF Marine Corps"), [Special Forces](/wiki/Special_Forces_(Maldives) "Special Forces (Maldives)"), Service Corps, Defence Intelligence Service, Military Police, Corps of Engineers, Special Protection Group, Medical Corps, Adjutant General's Corps, Air Corps, and Fire and Rescue Service. Maldives has an arrangement with India allowing cooperation on radar coverage.

As a water-bound nation, much of its security concerns life at sea. Almost 99% of the country is covered by sea and the remaining 1% land is scattered over an area of 800 km (497 mi) × 120 km (75 mi), with the largest island being not more than 8 km2 (3 sq mi). Therefore, the duties assigned to the MNDF of maintaining surveillance over Maldives' waters and providing protection against foreign intruders poaching in the EEZ and territorial waters, are immense tasks from both logistical and economic viewpoints.
The Coast Guard plays a vital role in carrying out these functions. To provide timely security its patrol boats are stationed at various MNDF Regional Headquarters. The Coast Guard is also assigned to respond to maritime distress calls and to conduct search and rescue operations promptly.
In 2019, Maldives signed the UN [treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[153]](#cite_note-157)

### Law

See also: [Judiciary of the Maldives](/wiki/Judiciary_of_the_Maldives "Judiciary of the Maldives") and [Law enforcement in the Maldives](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_the_Maldives "Law enforcement in the Maldives")

According to the [Constitution of Maldives](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Maldives "Constitution of the Maldives"), "the judges are independent, and subject only to the Constitution and the law. When deciding matters on which the Constitution or the law is silent, judges must consider Islamic [Shari'ah](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia")".[[154]](#cite_note-158)

Islam is the official religion of Maldives and open practice of any other religion is forbidden.[[155]](#cite_note-159) The 2008 constitution says that the republic "is based on the principles of Islam" and that "no law contrary to any principle of Islam can be applied". Non-Muslims are prohibited from becoming citizens.[[156]](#cite_note-Hirschl2011-160)

The requirement to adhere to a particular religion and prohibition of public worship following other religions is contrary to Article 18 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") and Article 18 of the [International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights](/wiki/International_Covenant_on_Civil_and_Political_Rights "International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights") to which Maldives has recently become party[[157]](#cite_note-161) and was addressed in Maldives' reservation in adhering to the Covenant claiming that "The application of the principles set out in Article 18 of the Covenant shall be without prejudice to the Constitution of the Republic of Maldives."[[158]](#cite_note-162)

A new [penal code](/wiki/Criminal_code "Criminal code") came into effect on 16 July 2015, replacing the 1968 law, the first modern, comprehensive penal code to incorporate the major tenets and principles of Islamic law.[[159]](#cite_note-163)[[160]](#cite_note-164)

[Same-sex relations](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") are illegal in Maldives, although tourist resorts typically operate as exceptions to this law.[[161]](#cite_note-165)[[162]](#cite_note-166)[[163]](#cite_note-167)

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in the Maldives](/wiki/Human_rights_in_the_Maldives "Human rights in the Maldives")

Human rights in Maldives is a contentious issue. In its 2011 [Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World") report, [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") declared Maldives "Partly Free", claiming a reform process that had made headway in 2009 and 2010 had stalled.[[164]](#cite_note-FH_2011-168) The [United States Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights and Labor](/wiki/Bureau_of_Democracy,_Human_Rights,_and_Labor "Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor") claims in their 2012 report on human rights practices in the country that the most significant problems are corruption, lack of [religious freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion"), abuse, and unequal treatment of women.[[165]](#cite_note-State_2012-169)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the Maldives](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Maldives "Economy of the Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/The_western_side_%28382669110%29.jpg/250px-The_western_side_%28382669110%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_western_side_(382669110).jpg)

Malé harbour

Historically, Maldives provided enormous quantities of [cowry](/wiki/Cowry "Cowry") shells, [an international currency of the early ages](/wiki/History_of_money "History of money"). From the 2nd century CE, the islands were known as the 'Money Isles' by the Arabs.[[166]](#cite_note-170) *[Monetaria moneta](/wiki/Monetaria_moneta "Monetaria moneta")* was used for centuries as a currency in Africa, and huge amounts of [Maldivian](/wiki/Maldive_Islands "Maldive Islands") cowries were introduced into Africa by western nations during the period of [slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade").[[167]](#cite_note-171) The cowry is now the symbol of Maldives [Monetary Authority](/wiki/Maldives_Monetary_Authority "Maldives Monetary Authority").

In the early 1970s and 1980s, Maldives was one of the world's 20 poorest countries, with a population of 100,000.[[168]](#cite_note-172) The economy at the time was largely dependent on fisheries and trading local goods such as [coir](/wiki/Coir "Coir") rope, [ambergris](/wiki/Ambergris "Ambergris") (Maavaharu), and [coco de mer](/wiki/Coco_de_mer "Coco de mer") (Tavakkaashi) with neighbouring countries and [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian "East Asian") countries.[[169]](#cite_note-173)

The Maldivian government began a largely successful [economic reform](/wiki/Economic_reform "Economic reform") programme in the 1980s, initiated by lifting import quotas and giving more opportunities to the private sector. At that time, the tourism sector was in its infant stage but would later play a significant role in the nation's development. Agriculture and manufacturing continue to play lesser roles in the economy, constrained by the limited availability of cultivable land and the shortage of domestic labour.

### Tourism

Main articles: [Tourism in the Maldives](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_Maldives "Tourism in the Maldives"), [Diving in the Maldives](/wiki/Diving_in_the_Maldives "Diving in the Maldives"), and [List of mosques in the Maldives](/wiki/List_of_mosques_in_the_Maldives "List of mosques in the Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Filitheyo_bar.jpg/250px-Filitheyo_bar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Filitheyo_bar.jpg)

Filitheyo island beach with tall palm trees and blue fresh lagoons

Maldives remained largely unknown to tourists until the early 1970s. Only 200 islands are home to its 382,751 inhabitants.[[170]](#cite_note-174)[[171]](#cite_note-175) The other islands are used entirely for economic purposes, of which tourism and agriculture are the most dominant. [Tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_Maldives "Tourism in the Maldives") accounts for 28% of the GDP and more than 60% of Maldives' foreign exchange receipts. Over 90% of government tax revenue comes from import duties and tourism-related taxes.

The development of tourism fostered the overall growth of the [country's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Maldives "Economy of the Maldives"). It created direct and indirect employment and income generation opportunities in other related industries. The first tourist resorts were opened in 1972 with Bandos Island Resort and Kurumba Village (the current name is Kurumba Maldives),[[172]](#cite_note-176) which transformed Maldives' economy.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Landaa_Giraavaru_vue_du_ciel.JPG/250px-Landaa_Giraavaru_vue_du_ciel.JPG)](/wiki/File:Landaa_Giraavaru_vue_du_ciel.JPG)

The [resort island](/wiki/Resort_island "Resort island") of [Landaa Giraavaru](/wiki/Landaa_Giraavaru "Landaa Giraavaru") (Baa atoll)

According to the [Ministry of Tourism](/wiki/Ministry_of_Tourism_(Maldives) "Ministry of Tourism (Maldives)"), the emergence of tourism in 1972 transformed the economy, moving rapidly from dependence on fisheries to tourism. In just three and a half decades, the industry became the main source of income. Tourism was also the country's biggest foreign currency earner and the single largest contributor to the GDP. As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maldives&action=edit), 89 resorts in Maldives offered over 17,000 beds and hosted over 600,000 tourists annually.[[173]](#cite_note-177) In 2019, over 1.7 million visitors came to the islands.[[174]](#cite_note-178)

The number of resorts increased from 2 to 92 between 1972 and 2007. As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maldives&action=edit), over 8,380,000 tourists had visited Maldives.[[175]](#cite_note-179)

The country has [six heritage Maldivian coral mosques](/wiki/List_of_mosques_in_the_Maldives "List of mosques in the Maldives") listed as [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") tentative sites.[[176]](#cite_note-180)

#### Visitors

See also: [§ Transportation](#Transportation), and [Visa policy of Maldives](/wiki/Visa_policy_of_Maldives "Visa policy of Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Maldives_Airport-Maldives-Andres_Larin.jpg/250px-Maldives_Airport-Maldives-Andres_Larin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maldives_Airport-Maldives-Andres_Larin.jpg)

Sign outside the Velana International Airport

Visitors to Maldives do not need to apply for a visa pre-arrival, regardless of their country of origin, provided they have a passport with at least 1 month validity, a complete [travel itinerary](/wiki/Travel_itinerary "Travel itinerary") (including return journey confirmed tickets, prepaid confirmed booking at a registered facility,[[177]](#cite_note-181) proof of financial means for sufficient funds to support the stay in Maldives, and entry requirements for their onward destinations—such as visa and passport validity), a Traveller Declaration form (which must be filled out and submitted by all foreigners arriving in Maldives within 96 hours of the flight), and a Yellow Fever Vaccination Certificate, if applicable.[[178]](#cite_note-182) However, entry is denied to Israeli passports.[[179]](#cite_note-doi-183)

Most visitors arrive at [Velana International Airport](/wiki/Velana_International_Airport "Velana International Airport"), on [Hulhulé](/wiki/Hulhul%C3%A9 "Hulhulé") Island, adjacent to the capital Malé. The airport is served by flights to and from [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha"), [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai"), [Abu Dhabi](/wiki/Abu_Dhabi "Abu Dhabi"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Dhaka](/wiki/Dhaka "Dhaka"), [Istanbul](/wiki/Istanbul "Istanbul"), and major airports in [South-East Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") like [Kuala Lumpur International](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_International_Airport "Kuala Lumpur International Airport") in Malaysia, as well as charters from Europe like [Charles De Gaulle](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_Airport "Charles de Gaulle Airport") in France. [Gan Airport](/wiki/Gan_International_Airport "Gan International Airport"), on the southern atoll of Addu, also serves an international flight to [Malpensa](/wiki/Milan_Malpensa_Airport "Milan Malpensa Airport") in Milan several times a week. [British Airways](/wiki/British_Airways "British Airways") offers direct flights to Maldives from [Heathrow Airport](/wiki/Heathrow_Airport "Heathrow Airport").[[180]](#cite_note-184)

### Fishing industry

Main article: [Fishing industry in the Maldives](/wiki/Fishing_industry_in_the_Maldives "Fishing industry in the Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Doni_aux_Maldives_cropped.jpg/250px-Doni_aux_Maldives_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Doni_aux_Maldives_cropped.jpg)

A mechanised traditional inter-island dhoni stripped of its sails

For many centuries the Maldivian economy was entirely dependent on fishing and other [marine](/wiki/Ocean "Ocean") products. Fishing remains the main occupation of the people and the government gives priority to the fisheries sector.

The [mechanisation](/wiki/Mechanisation "Mechanisation") of the [traditional fishing boat](/wiki/Traditional_fishing_boat "Traditional fishing boat") called *[dhoni](/wiki/Dhoni_(fishing_vessel) "Dhoni (fishing vessel)")* in 1974 was a major milestone in the development of the fisheries industry. A fish canning plant was installed on [Felivaru](/wiki/Felivaru "Felivaru") in 1977, as a joint venture with a Japanese firm. In 1979, a Fisheries Advisory Board was set up with the mandate of advising the government on policy guidelines for the overall development of the fisheries sector. Manpower development programmes began in the early 1980s, and fisheries education was incorporated into the school curriculum. [Fish aggregating devices](/wiki/Fish_aggregating_device "Fish aggregating device") and navigational aids were located at various strategic points. Moreover, the opening up of the [exclusive economic zone](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone "Exclusive economic zone") (EEZ) of Maldives for fisheries has further enhanced the growth of the fisheries sector.

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maldives&action=edit), fisheries contributed over 15% of the country's GDP and engaged about 30% of the country's workforce. Fisheries were also the second-largest foreign exchange earner after [tourism](#Tourism).

### Transportation

Main articles: [Transport in the Maldives](/wiki/Transport_in_the_Maldives "Transport in the Maldives") and [List of airports in the Maldives](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_the_Maldives "List of airports in the Maldives")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Velana_International_Airport.png/250px-Velana_International_Airport.png)](/wiki/File:Velana_International_Airport.png)

Velana International Airport

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Seaplane_Terminal.png/250px-Seaplane_Terminal.png)](/wiki/File:Seaplane_Terminal.png)

Seaplane Terminal

[Velana International Airport](/wiki/Velana_International_Airport "Velana International Airport") is the principal gateway to Maldives; it is adjacent to the capital city Malé and is connected by [Sinamalé Bridge](/wiki/Sinamal%C3%A9_Bridge "Sinamalé Bridge"). International travel is available on government-owned [Island Aviation Services](/wiki/Island_Aviation_Services "Island Aviation Services") (branded as Maldivian), which operates [DHC-6 Twin Otter](/wiki/DHC-6_Twin_Otter "DHC-6 Twin Otter") seaplanes and to nearly all Maldivian domestic airports with several [Bombardier Dash 8](/wiki/Bombardier_Dash_8 "Bombardier Dash 8") aircraft, and one [Airbus A320](/wiki/Airbus_A320 "Airbus A320") with international service to India, Bangladesh, Sri Lanka, Malaysia, Thailand, and China.

In Maldives, there are three main ways to travel between islands: by domestic flight, by [seaplane](/wiki/Seaplane "Seaplane"), or by boat.[[181]](#cite_note-185) For several years two seaplane companies were operating: TMA ([Trans Maldivian Airways](/wiki/Trans_Maldivian_Airways "Trans Maldivian Airways")) and [Maldivian Air Taxi](/wiki/Maldivian_Air_Taxi "Maldivian Air Taxi"), but these merged in 2013 under the name TMA. The seaplane fleet is entirely made up of DHC-6 Twin Otters. There is also another airline, [Villa Air](/wiki/Flyme_(Villa_Air) "Flyme (Villa Air)"), which operates using [ATR](/wiki/ATR_(aircraft_manufacturer) "ATR (aircraft manufacturer)") planes to domestic airports, principally [Villa-Maamigili](/wiki/Villa_International_Airport_Maamigili "Villa International Airport Maamigili"), [Dharavandhoo](/wiki/Dharavandhoo_Airport "Dharavandhoo Airport") and some others. [Manta Air](/wiki/Manta_Air "Manta Air") began its first scheduled seaplane service in 2019. Its seaplane fleet is made up of DHC-6 Twin Otter aircraft. In addition to the seaplane service, [Manta Air](/wiki/Manta_Air "Manta Air") uses [ATR 72–600](/wiki/ATR_(aircraft_manufacturer) "ATR (aircraft manufacturer)") aircraft to operate domestic flights to [Dhaalu Airport](/wiki/Dhaalu_Airport "Dhaalu Airport"), [Dharavandhoo Airport](/wiki/Dharavandhoo_Airport "Dharavandhoo Airport") and [Kooddoo Airport](/wiki/Kooddoo_Airport "Kooddoo Airport") from the main [Velana International Airport](/wiki/Velana_International_Airport "Velana International Airport").[[182]](#cite_note-186) Depending on the distance of the destination island from the airport, resorts organise [speedboat](/wiki/Speedboat "Speedboat") transfers or [seaplane](/wiki/Seaplane "Seaplane") flights directly to the resort island jetty for their guests. Several daily flights operate from [Velana International Airport](/wiki/Velana_International_Airport "Velana International Airport") to the 18 domestic and international airports in the country. Scheduled ferries also operate from Malé to many of the atolls. The traditional Maldivian boat is called a [dhoni](/wiki/Dhoni_(fishing_vessel) "Dhoni (fishing vessel)"), one of the oldest known sea vessels in Maldives.[[183]](#cite_note-187) Speedboats and seaplanes tend to be more expensive, while travel by dhoni, although slower, is relatively cheaper and convenient.

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of the Maldives](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Maldives "Demographics of the Maldives")

The largest and the only ethnic group in the country are [Maldivians](/wiki/Maldivians "Maldivians") (*Dhivehin*), native to the historic region of the Maldive Islands comprising today's Republic of Maldives and the island of [Minicoy](/wiki/Minicoy "Minicoy") in [Union territory of Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep"), India. They share the same culture and speak the [Dhivehi language](/wiki/Dhivehi_language "Dhivehi language"). They are principally an [Indo-Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples") people, having traces of Middle Eastern, South Asian, [Austronesian](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") and African genes in the population.

In the past, there was also a small [Tamil](/wiki/Tamils "Tamils") population known as the [Giraavaru people](/wiki/Giraavaru_people "Giraavaru people"). This group has now been almost completely assimilated into the larger Maldivian society but were native to the island of [Giraavaru](/wiki/Giraavaru_(Kaafu_Atoll) "Giraavaru (Kaafu Atoll)"), which was evacuated in 1968 due to heavy erosion of the island.[[187]](#cite_note-191) A small [Sinhalese](/wiki/Sinhalese_people "Sinhalese people") population in Maldives also made up about 0.7% of the total population in 2000.[[188]](#cite_note-192)

Some social stratification exists on the islands. It is not rigid, since rank is based on varied factors, including occupation, wealth, Islamic virtue, and family ties. Instead of a complex [caste](/wiki/Caste "Caste") system, there was merely a distinction between noble (bēfulhu) and common people in Maldives. Members of the social elite are concentrated in Malé.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Life_expectancy_by_WBG_-Maldives.png/250px-Life_expectancy_by_WBG_-Maldives.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_by_WBG_-Maldives.png)

Life expectancy in Maldives

The population doubled by 1978, and the [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth") rate peaked at 3.4% in 1985. By the 2006 census, the population had reached 298,968,[[189]](#cite_note-193) although the census in 2000 showed that the population growth rate had declined to 1.9%. [Life expectancy at birth](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") stood at 46 years in 1978, and later rose to 72. Infant mortality has declined from 12.7% in 1977 to 1.2% today, and adult literacy reached 99%. Combined school enrolment reached the high 90s. The population was projected to have reached 317,280 in 2010.[[190]](#cite_note-194)

The 2022 Population, Housing and Economic Census listed the total population in Maldives as 515,132 of which 382,639 were resident Maldivians and 132,493 were resident foreigners, with the foreign-born population comprising 26% of the total population. The six largest nationalities among migrants were Bangladeshi (56.4 percent), Indian (24.9 percent), Sri Lankan (8.6 percent), Nepali (3.9 percent), Indonesian (1.7 percent), and Filipino (1.3 percent).[[191]](#cite_note-:0-195)[[192]](#cite_note-196)

An increasing share of the Maldivian population is concentrated in Male. In 2022, approximately 41% of the population resided in the capital, compared to just 27% in 2000.[[191]](#cite_note-:0-195)

### Languages

See also: [Maldivian language](/wiki/Maldivian_language "Maldivian language")

The official and national language is [Dhivehi](/wiki/Maldivian_language "Maldivian language"), an [Indo-Aryan language](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") closely related to the [Sinhala language](/wiki/Sinhala_language "Sinhala language") of Sri Lanka. The first known script used to write Dhivehi is the *[eveyla akuru](/wiki/Eveyla_akuru "Eveyla akuru")* script, which is found in the historical recording of kings (*raadhavalhi*). Later a script called *[Dhives Akuru](/wiki/Dhives_Akuru "Dhives Akuru")* was used for a long period. The present-day script is called [Thaana](/wiki/Thaana "Thaana") and is written from right to left. [Thaana](/wiki/Thaana "Thaana") is derived from a mix of the old indigenous script of [Dhives Akuru](/wiki/Dhives_Akuru "Dhives Akuru") and [Arabic abjad](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script"). Thaana is said to have been introduced during the reign of [Mohamed Thakurufaanu](/wiki/Muhammad_Thakurufaanu_al-Auzam "Muhammad Thakurufaanu al-Auzam").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Dhivehiscript.svg/250px-Dhivehiscript.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Dhivehiscript.svg)

Thaana script

English is widely spoken by the locals of Maldives:[[193]](#cite_note-197) "Following the nation's opening to the outside world, the introduction of English as a medium of instruction at the secondary and tertiary levels of education, and its government's recognition of the opportunities offered through tourism, English has now firmly established itself in the country. [[194]](#cite_note-198)

Otherwise, [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") is taught in schools and mosques, as [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") is the [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"). The Maldivian population has formal or informal education in the reading, writing, and pronunciation of the Arabic language, as part of the compulsory religious education for all primary and secondary school students.[[1]](#cite_note-stategovreligion-1)

#### Thikijehi Thaana

These additional letters were added to the Thaana alphabet by adding dots ([*nukuthaa*](/wiki/Nuqta "Nuqta")) to existing letters, to allow for transliteration of Arabic loanwords, as previously Arabic loanwords were written using the Arabic script. Their usage is inconsistent, and becoming less frequent as the spelling changes to reflect pronunciation by Maldivians, rather than the original Arabic pronunciation, as the words get absorbed into the Maldivian language.

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in the Maldives](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Maldives "Religion in the Maldives")

After the long [Buddhist period of Maldivian history](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_Maldives "Buddhism in the Maldives"),[[195]](#cite_note-199) Muslim traders introduced Islam. Maldivians converted to Islam by the mid-12th century. The islands have had a long history of [Sufic](/wiki/Sufism "Sufism") orders, as can be seen in the history of the country such as the building of tombs. They were used until as recently as the 1980s for seeking the help of buried [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint"). They can be seen next to some old mosques and are considered a part of Maldives's [cultural heritage](/wiki/Cultural_heritage "Cultural heritage").

Other aspects of [tassawuf](/wiki/Tassawuf "Tassawuf"), such as ritualised [dhikr](/wiki/Dhikr "Dhikr") ceremonies called Maulūdu ([Mawlid](/wiki/Mawlid "Mawlid")) – the [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") of which included recitations and certain supplications in a melodic tone – existed until very recent times. These Maulūdu festivals were held in ornate tents specially built for the occasion.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At present Islam is the official religion of the entire population, as adherence to it is required for citizenship.[[1]](#cite_note-stategovreligion-1)

According to Arab traveller [Ibn Battuta](/wiki/Ibn_Battuta "Ibn Battuta"), the person responsible for this conversion was a Sunni Muslim visitor named Abu al-Barakat Yusuf al-Barbari, sailing from what is today [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"). He is also referred to as Tabrizugefaanu. His venerated tomb now stands on the grounds of Medhu Ziyaaraiy, across the street from the Friday Mosque, or [Hukuru Miskiy](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9_Friday_Mosque "Malé Friday Mosque"), in Malé. Originally built in 1153 and re-built in 1658,[[56]](#cite_note-Yoosuf-60) this is one of the country's oldest surviving mosques.

[Felix Wilfred](/wiki/Felix_Wilfred "Felix Wilfred") notes that the number of Christians in Maldives as 1,400 or 0.4% of the country's population.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

Since the adoption of the [2008 constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Maldives#2008_Constitution "Constitution of the Maldives"), Islam is established as the country's state religion and Maldivian citizens are required by law to follow [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam").[[1]](#cite_note-stategovreligion-1) However, in 2020, independent studies found that approximately 0.29% of the population is Christian (roughly split between [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") and [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant")).[[197]](#cite_note-201)

### Education

Main article: [Education in the Maldives](/wiki/Education_in_the_Maldives "Education in the Maldives")

The [Maldives National University](/wiki/Maldives_National_University "Maldives National University") is one of the country's institutions of higher education.[[e]](#cite_note-202)[[198]](#cite_note-203) In 1973, the Allied Health Services Training Centre (the forerunner of the Faculty of Health Sciences) was established by the [Ministry of Health](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_(Maldives) "Ministry of Health (Maldives)").[[199]](#cite_note-204) The Vocational Training Centre was established in 1974, providing training for mechanical and electrical trades.[[200]](#cite_note-205) In 1984, the Institute for Teacher Education was created and the School of Hotel and Catering Services was established in 1987 to provide trained personnel for the tourist industry.[[201]](#cite_note-206) In 1991, the Institute of Management and Administration was created to train staff for public and private services. In 1998, Maldives College of Higher Education was founded. The Institute of Shar'ah and Law was founded in January 1999. In 2000 the college launched its first-degree programme, Bachelor of Arts. On 17 January 2011, Maldives National University Act was passed by the President of Maldives; Maldives National University was named on 15 February 2011. In 2015 under a Presidential decree the College of Islamic Studies was changed into the Islamic University of Maldives (IUM).[[202]](#cite_note-207)

The Maldivian government now offers 3 different scholarships to students who have completed their higher secondary education with results above a certain threshold, with ranks of the scholarship received depending on the merits achieved by students on their year 12 examinations.[[203]](#cite_note-208)

### Health

Main article: [Health in the Maldives](/wiki/Health_in_the_Maldives "Health in the Maldives")

The Human Rights Measurement Initiative reports that Maldives is meeting 5.1 out of 10 of the expected fulfilment for the right to health considering its income level.[[204]](#cite_note-Hrm-209) Specifically for children's health rights, Maldives attains 98.0% of the anticipated level based on its current income.[[204]](#cite_note-Hrm-209) Regarding adult health rights, the country achieves 99.7% of the expected fulfilment considering its income level. However, in terms of reproductive health rights, Maldives falls into the "very bad" category, as it fulfills only 18.2% of the expected achievement based on its available resources.[[205]](#cite_note-210)

[Life expectancy at birth](/wiki/Life_expectancy_at_birth "Life expectancy at birth") in Maldives was 77 years in 2011.[[206]](#cite_note-211) Infant mortality fell from 34 per 1,000 in 1990 to 15 in 2004. There is an increasing disparity between health in the capital and on the other islands. There is also a problem of [malnutrition](/wiki/Malnutrition "Malnutrition"). Imported food is expensive.[[207]](#cite_note-212)

On 24 May 2021, Maldives had the world's fastest-growing [COVID-19 outbreak](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), with the highest number of infections per million people over the prior 7 and 14 days, according to data compiled by [Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_News "Bloomberg News").[[208]](#cite_note-213) Doctors warned that increasing demand for COVID-19 care could hinder their ability to handle other health emergencies in Maldives.[[209]](#cite_note-214) The reason for the outbreak was the [Delta variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Delta_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Delta variant").

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of the Maldives](/wiki/Culture_of_the_Maldives "Culture of the Maldives")

See also: [Maldivian cuisine](/wiki/Maldivian_cuisine "Maldivian cuisine") and [Folklore of the Maldives](/wiki/Folklore_of_the_Maldives "Folklore of the Maldives")

The culture of Maldives is influenced by the cultures of the people of different ethnicities who have settled on the islands throughout the times.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Maahefun_Fuvahmulah.JPG/250px-Maahefun_Fuvahmulah.JPG)](/wiki/File:Maahefun_Fuvahmulah.JPG)

[Maahefun](/wiki/Maahefun "Maahefun") Festival in [Fuvahmulah](/wiki/Fuvahmulah "Fuvahmulah")

Since the 12th century AD, there were also influences from [Arabia](/wiki/Arabian_Peninsula "Arabian Peninsula") in the language and culture of Maldives because of the conversion to Islam and its location as a crossroads in the central Indian Ocean.[[210]](#cite_note-215) This was due to the long trading history between the far east and the middle east.

Reflective of this is the fact that Maldives has had the highest national divorce rate in the world for many decades. This, it is hypothesised, is due to a combination of liberal Islamic rules about divorce and the relatively loose marital bonds that have been identified as common in non- and semi-sedentary peoples without a history of fully developed agrarian property and kinship relations.[[211]](#cite_note-216)

### Media

Main articles: [Television Maldives](/wiki/Television_Maldives "Television Maldives"), [Voice of Maldives](/wiki/Voice_of_Maldives "Voice of Maldives"), and [List of newspapers in the Maldives](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_the_Maldives "List of newspapers in the Maldives")

[PSM news](/wiki/PSM_news "PSM news") serves as the country's main media, owned by the [government of Maldives](/wiki/Government_of_the_Maldives "Government of the Maldives").[[212]](#cite_note-217) The newspaper was founded on 3 May 2017, in the celebration of [World Press Freedom Day](/wiki/World_Press_Freedom_Day "World Press Freedom Day").[[213]](#cite_note-218) Maldives has been ranked one–hundred in the [World Press Freedom Index](/wiki/World_Press_Freedom_Index "World Press Freedom Index") 2023 and 106 in 2024.[[214]](#cite_note-219) The country's first daily newspaper, [Haveeru Daily](/wiki/Haveeru_Daily "Haveeru Daily") News was the first and longest–serving newspaper in the history of Maldives, which was registered on 28 December 1978, and dissolved in 2016.[[215]](#cite_note-220)
Article 28 of Maldives [Constitution](/wiki/Maldives_Constitution "Maldives Constitution") guarantees freedom of the press and stipulates that:

> No person shall be compelled to disclose the source of any information that is espoused, disseminated, or published by that person.[[216]](#cite_note-221)

However, this protection is compromised by the Evidence Act, which came into effect in January 2023 and grants courts the authority to compel journalists to reveal their confidential sources. Maldives Media Council (MMC) and Maldives [Journalists Association](/wiki/Maldives_Journalists_Association "Maldives Journalists Association") (MJA) serve as crucial watchdogs in addressing and combating these threats. Newspapers *Sun Online*, *[Mihaaru](/wiki/Mihaaru "Mihaaru")* and its English edition named *[The Edition](/wiki/The_Edition "The Edition"),* and *Avas* serve as well–known private news outlets.[[217]](#cite_note-222)

### Sports

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Maldives_%2815617923914%29.jpg/250px-Maldives_%2815617923914%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Maldives_(15617923914).jpg)

[Flyboarding](/wiki/Flyboard "Flyboard") in Maldives

Sports are deeply ingrained in the culture of Maldives, with various activities based on traditional pastimes and modern sporting pursuits. Given its geography of scattered islands surrounded by the Indian Ocean, [water sports](/wiki/Water_sports "Water sports") naturally hold a prominent position. [Surfing](/wiki/Surfing "Surfing"), in particular, has gained international recognition. Locations such as the atolls of North and South [Malé](/wiki/Mal%C3%A9 "Malé"), [Thulusdhoo](/wiki/Thulusdhoo "Thulusdhoo"), and [Himmafushi](/wiki/Himmafushi "Himmafushi") offer ideal surfing conditions.[[218]](#cite_note-223) Additionally, diving and snorkelling are immensely popular.[[219]](#cite_note-224)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Topu_Barman_in_action_against_Maldives_in_the_2021_SAFF_Championship.jpg/250px-Topu_Barman_in_action_against_Maldives_in_the_2021_SAFF_Championship.jpg)](/wiki/File:Topu_Barman_in_action_against_Maldives_in_the_2021_SAFF_Championship.jpg)

[Maldives](/wiki/Maldives_national_football_team "Maldives national football team") against [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh_national_football_team "Bangladesh national football team") at the [2021 SAFF Championship](/wiki/2021_SAFF_Championship "2021 SAFF Championship").

[Football in Maldives](/wiki/Football_in_the_Maldives "Football in the Maldives") is one of the most widely played and passionately-followed sports in the country.[[220]](#cite_note-225) Maldives [national football team](/wiki/Maldives_national_football_team "Maldives national football team") competes in regional and international tournaments. The country has its own domestic football league, the [Dhivehi Premier League](/wiki/Dhivehi_Premier_League "Dhivehi Premier League"), featuring clubs from various atolls vying for supremacy.[[221]](#cite_note-226) Matches often draw large crowds. [Futsal](/wiki/Futsal "Futsal") is also popular, especially among younger generations, with numerous indoor facilities providing spaces for friendly matches and competitive leagues.

Bodu Beru, a rhythmic drumming and [dance performance](/wiki/Dance_performance "Dance performance"), often accompanies events for traditional sports such as "Baibalaa", a game resembling [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball "Volleyball") but played with a woven ball made from dried [coconut palm](/wiki/Coconut_palm "Coconut palm") leaves, and "Fenei Bashi", a form of [wrestling](/wiki/Wrestling "Wrestling").

   The Maldives predominantly uses the Maldivian Rufiyaa (MVR) as its official currency. However, U.S dollars are commonly accepted in tourist establishments due to the high number of visitors from USD-based countries.
